proposals.title: Environmental Context Modifies the Relative Contribution of Heritable and Non-Heritable Factors to Blood Pressure Variance: A Multi-Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: While hypertension has a well-established genetic component, its heritability estimates vary widely across populations, suggesting a strong role for gene-environment interactions. The rising global burden of cardiovascular disease necessitates a clearer understanding of how modifiable environmental and lifestyle factors can outweigh genetic predisposition in specific contexts. This is critical for developing targeted, effective public health interventions beyond genetic risk scores.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the proportion of blood pressure variance explained by non-heritable factors (e.g., diet quality, physical activity, socioeconomic status) will be significantly greater than that explained by polygenic risk scores in populations exposed to specific environmental contexts, such as areas with low access to healthy food or high levels of environmental stressors.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing data from large prospective cohorts (e.g., EPIC-Norfolk, UK Biobank) with detailed phenotypic, genetic, and environmental data. We will construct a polygenic risk score (PRS) for blood pressure using established pre-2026 loci. Using multilevel mixed-effects models, we will partition systolic and diastolic blood pressure variance into components attributable to the PRS, measured non-heritable factors (from metabolomic and lifestyle data), and their interaction. Context will be defined using geospatial data on food environment, walkability, and area-level deprivation, allowing for stratified and interaction analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify the context-dependent balance of heritable and modifiable risk for hypertension, moving beyond static heritability estimates. It will identify specific environmental settings where lifestyle interventions could have the greatest marginal benefit, even for those at high genetic risk, informing precision public health strategies.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond simply estimating heritability or testing gene-environment interactions to directly compare the explanatory power of genetic versus non-genetic factors across defined environmental strata, challenging the primacy of genetics as the dominant risk narrative in all settings.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Modelling of Age-Dependent Polygenic Score Effects on Type 2 Diabetes Risk and Cardiometabolic Biomarkers: A Repeated-Measures Analysis in EPIC-Norfolk
proposals.motivation: Polygenic scores (PGS) for complex diseases like type 2 diabetes (T2D) are derived from cross-sectional genome-wide association studies (GWAS), assuming static genetic effects. However, evidence suggests genetic associations for cardiometabolic traits may vary with age. Understanding whether an individual's genetic risk changes over their life course is critical for accurate risk prediction and for identifying periods of heightened genetic susceptibility for targeted intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: The phenotypic expression of an individual's polygenic score for T2D and related cardiometabolic traits (e.g., HbA1c, fasting glucose) is not static but changes with chronological age and is modified by cumulative, time-varying environmental exposures, including diet quality and physical activity.
proposals.method: We will use repeated-measures data from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, with deep phenotypic data (biomarkers, diet, activity) collected over ~20 years. We will calculate PGS for T2D and related traits using pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics. Using linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts for participants, we will model the interaction between PGS and age (time) on longitudinal outcomes. We will extend models to include time-updated interaction terms between PGS and cumulative environmental scores (e.g., planetary health diet index, physical activity energy expenditure) derived from each participant's personal history, adjusting for key confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, within-individual evidence for the dynamic nature of genetic effects on T2D risk in a large, prospective cohort. It will generate novel insights into how lifestyle exposures cumulatively modify genetic susceptibility over time, moving beyond static risk prediction. The findings will inform more personalized, life-stage-specific prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond cross-sectional PGS-environment interaction studies by leveraging a longitudinal, repeated-measures framework within individuals, which inherently controls for between-person confounding and cohort effects, providing a direct test of intra-individual genetic effect dynamics.

proposals.title: Dynamic Polygenic Risk: A Bayesian State-Space Model to Estimate Age- and Exposure-Dependent Genetic Effects on Blood Pressure Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for blood pressure (BP) assume static genetic effects, yet BP trajectories and their associated cardiovascular risk vary across the lifespan. This static assumption may misrepresent the true biological interplay between genetics and time-varying environmental exposures, limiting the personalization and predictive utility of genetic risk assessment. Testing whether genetic effects are dynamic is crucial for understanding disease etiology and improving risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: The effect sizes of BP-associated genetic loci are not constant but vary as a function of age and cumulative environmental exposures, such as long-term smoking or air pollution.
proposals.method: We will apply a Bayesian state-space modeling framework, adapted from ecological time-series analysis, to rich longitudinal data (e.g., EPIC-Norfolk). The model will treat individual genetic effect sizes as latent variables that evolve over time according to specified functions of age and cumulative exposure. We will use Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (e.g., Stan) for inference, explicitly partitioning observation error in BP measurements from the underlying process dynamics of genetic effects, while adjusting for time-varying confounders like diet and medication use.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal test of the dynamic genetic effect hypothesis for BP, moving beyond static PRS. It will deliver a novel analytical framework for longitudinal genetic epidemiology and generate insights into critical periods when genetic risk is most potent, informing targeted prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in genetic epidemiology, directly translating methods from ecology to model genetic effects as latent, dynamic processes rather than fixed parameters, thereby rigorously separating true effect modification from measurement noise.

proposals.title: Testing the Static Genetic Effect Hypothesis: A Lifecourse Analysis of Blood Pressure Polygenic Score Associations in the EPIC-Norfolk Cohort
proposals.motivation: Polygenic scores (PGS) for blood pressure (BP) are widely used to assess genetic risk, but their effect sizes are typically estimated from cross-sectional studies, assuming they are constant over the lifecourse. This assumption may be flawed if genetic effects are modified by age or cumulative environmental exposures, such as diet quality or physical activity, which could have major implications for risk prediction and personalized prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect sizes of a BP polygenic score on measured systolic and diastolic blood pressure are not static but change with age and are modified by cumulative exposure to lifestyle factors, specifically diet quality and physical activity.
proposals.method: We will use data from the EPIC-Norfolk prospective cohort, leveraging repeated clinical measures (including BP) and detailed lifestyle questionnaires across multiple timepoints. We will construct a validated BP PGS using pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics. Using linear mixed-effects models, we will test for interactions between the PGS and (a) age at measurement and (b) time-varying, cumulative indices of diet quality (e.g., Planetary Health Diet Index) and physical activity. We will also employ stratified analyses by age decade and test for non-linear interactions using spline functions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct empirical evidence on the stability of genetic effects for a major cardiometabolic trait over time. It will clarify whether PGS-based risk prediction requires age- or exposure-specific calibration and identify subgroups where genetic predisposition is most strongly expressed, informing targeted public health interventions.
proposals.novelty: While gene-environment interactions are studied, explicitly testing the *static effect* assumption of a PGS across the adult lifespan within a deeply phenotyped cohort, using cumulative rather than single-point exposure metrics, is a novel and critical test of a foundational premise in genetic epidemiology.

proposals.title: The Hypertensive Epigenome: A Prospective Cohort Study to Identify Causal, Pressure-Induced DNA Methylation Signatures Associated with Cardiovascular Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: Hypertension is a major risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD), but its pathophysiological mechanisms are incompletely understood. While genetic and lifestyle factors influence blood pressure (BP), it remains unclear if chronic BP elevation itself directly induces stable epigenetic changes that contribute to disease persistence and progression, analogous to the biological memory of chronic stress. Establishing such a causal, reverse pathway would reframe hypertension's role in CVD etiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic exposure to elevated arterial blood pressure causally induces specific, stable alterations in DNA methylation (the 'pressure epigenome') in accessible tissues like blood, and that these epigenetic marks mediate part of the association between hypertension and subsequent cardiovascular disease risk.
proposals.method: We will leverage the deeply phenotyped EPIC-Norfolk cohort, applying a nested case-control design. Using pre-existing DNA samples, we will perform epigenome-wide association studies (EWAS) with the Illumina EPIC array, comparing methylation profiles between individuals who developed sustained hypertension during follow-up and matched normotensive controls. To infer causality, we will apply Mendelian Randomization (MR) using established genetic variants for BP as instrumental variables. Mediation analysis will test if identified methylation signatures statistically mediate the known association between hypertension and incident coronary heart disease (CHD).
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel, putatively causal epigenetic biomarkers of hypertensive exposure, moving beyond associative findings. It will provide mechanistic insight into how hypertension biologically embeds risk, potentially revealing new targets for intervention or strategies for risk stratification beyond traditional BP measurements.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the standard causal assumption by testing hypertension as an *exposure* that drives epigenetic change, not merely an outcome. It directly adapts the 'reverse epigenetics' model from psychoneuroimmunology to a major cardiometabolic condition, a novel conceptual translation.

proposals.title: Quantitative mapping of tissue-specific genetic leverage on systemic vascular function using inducible mouse models and high-resolution phenotyping
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies, including those from EPIC-Norfolk, consistently show strong associations between metabolic risk factors (e.g., diet, adiposity) and cardiovascular disease. However, these observational data cannot disentangle the causal, tissue-specific genetic pathways through which distant organs like the liver, kidney, or brain systemically influence vascular physiology. Understanding the quantitative contribution of specific tissues is crucial for developing targeted interventions to mitigate vascular risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discrete, adult-stage genetic perturbations in key metabolic or regulatory tissues (hepatocytes, renal tubule cells, specific neuronal populations) will have quantitatively distinct and measurable impacts on systemic vascular hemodynamics and mechanics, revealing a hierarchy of tissue-specific genetic leverage over vascular function.
proposals.method: We will employ tamoxifen-inducible, tissue-specific Cre-loxP systems in adult mice to conditionally knockout candidate genes (prioritized from human genetic and metabolomic studies of vascular disease) in hepatocytes (Alb-CreERT2), renal tubules (Pax8-CreERT2), and hypothalamic neurons (Agrp-CreERT2). Vascular physiology will be quantified via in vivo high-frequency ultrasound for hemodynamics (pulse wave velocity, cardiac output) and ex vivo pressure myography for mesenteric artery mechanics. A linear mixed-effects model will be used to compare the effect size of each tissue-specific perturbation on the vascular phenotypes, controlling for body weight and plasma metabolite levels (measured by targeted LC-MS).
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond association to establish a causal, quantitative map of how non-vascular tissues genetically regulate the vasculature. It will identify which organ systems exert the greatest leverage, providing a mechanistic framework for interpreting human genetic risk scores and informing the development of tissue-targeted therapies for vascular dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While tissue-specific knockouts are common, their application to quantitatively rank and compare the systemic vascular impact of adult-stage perturbations across multiple organ systems is novel, isolating direct physiological effects from developmental compensation.

proposals.title: Calibrating Polygenic Risk Scores for Cardiometabolic Disease Across Ancestries: A Multi-Cohort Framework Inspired by Ecological Niche Modeling
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for diseases like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, predominantly derived from European-ancestry genome-wide association studies (GWAS), show substantially reduced predictive performance in non-European populations. This inequity limits the clinical utility and equitable translation of genetic discoveries. The problem mirrors the transferability challenge in ecological niche modeling, where models fail in new environments due to unaccounted-for contextual differences.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a formal, multi-population calibration and validation framework, analogous to methods used in ecology, will identify specific genetic (e.g., LD structure, allele frequency) and modifiable lifestyle factors that limit PRS transferability, enabling the development of ancestry-calibrated models with improved and more equitable predictive performance.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing genetic, metabolite, and detailed lifestyle data from diverse cohorts, including EPIC-Norfolk and international partners. For cardiometabolic traits, we will calculate standard European-derived PRS and test them in parallel across ancestries, quantifying transferability decay via variance explained and effect size correlations. We will then employ gradient boosting and mixed-model algorithms to identify population-specific genetic architectures and gene-environment interactions (e.g., diet, physical activity) that act as 'limiting factors' for prediction. This diagnostic will inform the development of calibrated models using methods like PRS-CSx or ancestry-specific weighting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized framework for diagnosing and reporting PRS transferability, moving beyond simple replication. It will deliver a set of calibrated, multi-ancestry PRS for cardiometabolic disease, explicitly validated for use across a diversity spectrum. The findings will directly inform guidelines for the equitable implementation of genetic risk prediction in global health.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly adapting the conceptual and methodological framework of ecological niche model calibration to human genetics, focusing on parallel multi-cohort diagnosis rather than sequential replication. It integrates genetic and pre-2026 high-resolution lifestyle/metabolite data to dissect the causes of transferability failure.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Closed-Loop, Context-Aware JITAI for Sustaining Lifestyle Modification in Adults with High Polygenic Risk for Hypertension
proposals.motivation: Despite robust evidence linking lifestyle to hypertension risk, long-term adherence to healthy behaviours remains poor, particularly for individuals at high genetic risk. Static lifestyle advice, as often delivered in cohort studies, shows limited long-term efficacy. Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (JITAIs) offer a promising, data-driven alternative by providing support at moments of vulnerability, but their long-term (≥24-month) effectiveness for chronic disease prevention in high-risk populations is untested.
proposals.hypothesis: A smartphone-delivered JITAI that uses continuous sensor data to trigger personalized, context-sensitive micro-interventions will lead to greater sustained engagement and more favourable changes in modifiable risk factors (e.g., physical activity, diet quality) over 24 months in adults with high polygenic risk for hypertension, compared to a static digital education platform.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month RCT in 500 EPIC-Norfolk participants with high polygenic risk scores for hypertension. The intervention arm uses a platform integrating data from a wrist-worn accelerometer and heart rate monitor (e.g., Fitbit) and smartphone sensors (GPS, usage). A central server will run a reinforcement learning algorithm to personalize intervention timing and type (e.g., stress-reduction prompt, activity suggestion) based on individual context (location, time, activity state) and prior response history. The control arm receives a static app with educational content mirroring prior EPIC-based lifestyle advice. Primary outcomes are platform engagement metrics and change in a composite lifestyle score; secondary outcomes include blood pressure and novel metabolites linked to diet and activity.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first long-term evidence on the efficacy of a closed-loop JITAI for chronic disease prevention in a genetically defined high-risk group. It will advance methodological understanding of how to model and use real-world contextual data to sustain behaviour change. Findings will directly inform scalable, personalised prevention strategies that could offset genetic risk.
proposals.novelty: This integrates a pre-2026 polygenic risk score for precise participant stratification with a dynamically learning JITAI architecture, moving beyond one-size-fits-all or simple rule-based digital interventions to a truly adaptive, closed-loop system tested over a clinically relevant timeframe.

proposals.title: A Precision Prevention Trial for Hypertension: Testing the Efficacy of a Genetically-Informed, Multi-Modal Lifestyle Intervention Bundle (The HM-GRA Trial)
proposals.motivation: Hypertension is a leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease and remains highly prevalent. Current primary prevention strategies are often one-size-fits-all, leading to suboptimal efficacy. The successful Caries Management by Risk Assessment (CAMBRA) model in dentistry demonstrates the potential of risk-stratified, bundled interventions, but this approach has not been rigorously adapted for hypertension prevention using modern genetic and metabolic risk profiling.
proposals.hypothesis: In young adults at high genetic risk for hypertension, a targeted, intensive, and multi-modal lifestyle intervention bundle, delivered early, will significantly attenuate the rise in systolic blood pressure over time compared to standard preventive advice, thereby delaying or preventing the onset of clinical hypertension.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial in 800 normotensive adults aged 25-35. Participants will be stratified using a pre-2025 polygenic risk score (PRS) for hypertension, derived from large-scale genome-wide association studies. Those in the top 20% risk tier will be randomized to intervention or control. The intervention is a 24-month bundled program including: 1) personalized dietary advice based on metabolite profiling (targeting sodium, potassium, and branched-chain amino acids), 2) a structured physical activity prescription with accelerometry feedback, and 3) stress reduction training. The primary outcome is the change in 24-hour ambulatory systolic blood pressure from baseline to 24 months. Analysis will use a linear mixed-effects model adjusting for baseline covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-level evidence for the efficacy of a genetics-guided, multi-domain lifestyle intervention for primary hypertension prevention. It will generate a novel, actionable protocol (HM-GRA) for clinical practice and yield insights into how lifestyle modifies genetic risk trajectories, directly informing the field of precision public health.
proposals.novelty: This proposal innovatively transposes a validated clinical framework from dentistry (CAMBRA) to cardiometabolic disease and uniquely combines a hypertension PRS with dynamic metabolite profiling to personalize and deliver a bundled lifestyle intervention at a very early, pre-clinical stage.

proposals.title: Mendelian randomization and metabolomic profiling to identify reverse-causal pathways from subclinical coronary heart disease to kidney function for therapeutic target prioritization
proposals.motivation: Cardiorenal syndrome is a major clinical burden, but therapeutic strategies remain largely organ-specific. Observational studies, including those from EPIC, consistently show associations between cardiovascular risk factors and declining kidney function, yet the causal direction and mediating biological pathways are poorly understood. Identifying and validating the specific signals through which early CHD pathology drives renal impairment would enable a paradigm shift towards intercepting this disease axis.
proposals.hypothesis: Subclinical CHD, proxied by genetic risk scores and imaging biomarkers, causally reduces estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) through specific, identifiable circulating metabolic and inflammatory pathways, and these mediators represent druggable targets for kidney protection.
proposals.method: We will first perform bidirectional two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) using large-scale GWAS summary statistics for CHD traits and eGFR to establish causal direction. In the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, we will then integrate subclinical CHD measures (e.g., coronary artery calcium scores), serial eGFR, and high-throughput metabolomic and proteomic profiling (Nightingale, Olink) to identify candidate mediating molecules. We will apply multivariable MR and mediation analysis to validate these mediators within a causal inference framework. Finally, we will prioritize targets by intersecting validated mediators with druggable gene databases (e.g., DrugBank) and genetic evidence supporting safety via phenome-wide association studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal epidemiological map of heart-to-kidney signaling, moving beyond association to mechanism. It will generate a shortlist of high-confidence, genetically-supported therapeutic targets for drug discovery programs aimed at cardiorenal protection. The findings will also refine risk prediction by incorporating reverse-causal pathways into models for kidney disease progression.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in explicitly testing and exploiting the *reverse* causal direction (from heart to kidney) for target discovery, whereas most research assumes kidney dysfunction drives heart disease. It uniquely combines genetic epidemiology with deep molecular phenotyping in a well-characterized cohort to transition from observing associations to defining a treatable pathological axis.

proposals.title: Cell-type-resolved causal networks for cardiometabolic disease: integrating deconvolved multi-omics with Mendelian Randomization in the EPIC-Norfolk cohort
proposals.motivation: Bulk tissue multi-omics studies, while valuable, obscure the cell-type-specific molecular architecture of disease, limiting mechanistic insight. For complex cardiometabolic diseases like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, risk-associated genetic variants and molecular pathways likely operate in specific cellular contexts, but current integrative models cannot resolve this. This gap hinders the development of precise, biologically interpretable risk scores and targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying causal inference models to cell-type-resolved estimates of epigenomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic layers will reveal distinct, cell-type-specific causal networks linking genetic variants to molecular intermediates and incident cardiometabolic disease, which are masked in bulk-tissue analyses.
proposals.method: We will computationally deconvolve existing and new EPIC-Norfolk blood-based epigenomic (DNA methylation arrays), transcriptomic (RNA-seq), and proteomic (SOMAscan) data into cell-type proportions using pre-2026 reference-based methods (e.g., CIBERSORTx, EpiDISH). For each major immune cell type, we will generate cell-type-specific quantitative trait locus (QTL) maps. Two-sample Mendelian Randomization (MR) and multivariable MR will then be applied strictly within matched cell types to infer causal relationships between molecular layers (e.g., methylation -> gene expression -> protein) and from these layers to disease risk, using established GWAS summary statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first cell-type-aware causal molecular networks for cardiometabolic disease from a prospective cohort, directly linking genetic risk to actionable cellular mechanisms. It will advance beyond associative 'omics' integration by establishing putative causal pathways within specific biological contexts, thereby refining polygenic risk prediction and identifying cell-type-specific therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the sequential application of computational deconvolution to multiple 'omics' layers prior to causal network inference, moving beyond bulk-tissue MR or integration of single-cell data from separate studies, which remains scarce for large prospective cohorts like EPIC-Norfolk as of 2025.

proposals.title: Integration of genetic, epigenetic, and proteomic data to refine lifestyle-based risk scores for type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, lifestyle-based risk scores have proven valuable for predicting cardiometabolic diseases, but they capture only part of the causal pathway. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous risk loci, yet the biological mechanisms linking these variants to disease, and how they interact with modifiable lifestyle factors, remain largely opaque. A multi-omics approach is needed to bridge this gap and move from statistical association to mechanistic insight.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating genetic risk variants with tissue-specific epigenetic (DNA methylation) and proteomic data will identify key molecular pathways that mediate the association between lifestyle factors (diet, physical activity) and incident type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, leading to more biologically informed and precise risk prediction models.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing data from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, including genetic data, stored blood samples, and detailed lifestyle phenotyping. Using pre-2026 platforms, we will perform targeted DNA methylation profiling (e.g., Illumina EPIC array) and high-throughput proteomics (e.g., Olink or SOMAscan panels) on baseline samples from incident cases and matched controls. We will employ Mendelian Randomization to infer causality between molecular traits and disease, and use multi-omics integration methods like Multi-Omics Factor Analysis (MOFA) to identify latent factors linking genetic loci, epigenetic changes, protein levels, and lifestyle exposures.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel, biologically-annotated risk score that incorporates modifiable lifestyle factors with their underlying molecular mediators. It will identify specific proteins and methylation sites that are causal links in the lifestyle-disease pathway, providing actionable targets for intervention. The findings will advance the field from prediction to mechanistic understanding of how lifestyle influences disease risk.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have linked single omics layers to disease, this proposal uniquely integrates genetic, epigenetic, and proteomic data within a single, deeply phenotyped prospective cohort to explicitly model how lifestyle factors operate through these molecular pathways, moving beyond simple association to causal mediation.

proposals.title: A Triangulated Pipeline for Drug Repurposing: Integrating Mendelian Randomization, Experimental Metabolomics, and Real-World Clinical Data to De-Risk Candidates for Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Drug development is costly and slow, while many existing drugs have potential for new indications. Pre-2026, genetic studies like Mendelian Randomization (MR) have identified promising drug-disease links, but these findings often lack experimental validation and real-world evidence, leading to high failure rates in subsequent trials. A robust, multi-evidence pipeline is urgently needed to prioritize repurposing candidates with higher confidence for clinical investment.
proposals.hypothesis: A sequential pipeline integrating genetically-prioritized drug targets, experimental validation of their effects on disease-relevant metabolic pathways, and analysis of real-world clinical outcomes will identify and de-risk viable drug repurposing candidates for type 2 diabetes more effectively than genetic evidence alone.
proposals.method: We will first perform two-sample MR using large-scale genetic consortia data (e.g., UK Biobank, DIAGRAM) to identify genetically-supported drug targets for T2D. For top candidates, we will conduct experimental validation using high-throughput metabolite profiling in human pancreatic islet or hepatocyte cell lines treated with the drug, assessing changes in pathways like glycolysis or lipogenesis. Finally, we will analyze linked primary care (CPRD) and hospital (HES) records to compare disease incidence and safety profiles in patients prescribed the candidate drug for its original indication versus matched controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, publicly available framework for systematic drug repurposing that moves beyond association to establish causality and preliminary human efficacy. It will deliver a shortlist of de-risked, mechanistically supported candidate drugs for T2D, complete with estimates of real-world effect size and safety signals, ready for definitive clinical trial design.
proposals.novelty: This approach adversarially strengthens MR findings by directly testing their mechanistic plausibility in experimental models and then interrogating real-world clinical data, creating a uniquely robust triangulation of evidence rarely combined in pre-2026 repurposing research.

proposals.title: A Knowledge-Constrained, Explainable AI Framework for Discovering and Validating Polygenic and Lifestyle Interactions in Blood Pressure: An Application to the EPIC-Norfolk and UK Biobank Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Despite known polygenic risk scores and lifestyle factors for hypertension, a large proportion of blood pressure variance remains unexplained, likely due to complex, non-linear gene-gene (GxG) and gene-environment (GxE) interactions. Current machine learning methods for detecting these interactions are prone to false discovery and produce 'black-box' models with limited biological interpretability, hindering translation into mechanistic insight or clinical utility. This gap impedes the development of more precise risk stratification and targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-stage analytical framework that constrains data-driven discovery with prior biological knowledge and statistical genetics principles will identify robust, biologically plausible, and interpretable high-order interactions influencing blood pressure, surpassing the predictive and explanatory power of standard additive models.
proposals.method: We will apply gradient boosting machines (XGBoost) and feed-forward neural networks to high-density genetic and detailed lifestyle/metabolite data from EPIC-Norfolk and UK Biobank to screen for high-order interaction candidates. Candidate interactions will be filtered using external biological networks (e.g., STRING for protein-protein interactions, GTEx for co-expression) and assessed for robustness via Mendelian Randomization and colocalization analyses where possible. Finally, we will employ tailored explainable AI (XAI) techniques, such as integrated gradients and interaction-specific SHAP values, to generate interpretable, non-linear models of the validated GxG and GxE interplay.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, validated set of polygenic interaction models for blood pressure, providing new mechanistic hypotheses for biological pathways. It will establish a rigorous, generalizable framework for constrained AI discovery in complex trait genetics, directly addressing reproducibility concerns. The explainable outputs will facilitate the development of next-generation, interactive risk scores that integrate genetic predisposition with modifiable lifestyle factors.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a constrained, hypothesis-guided screening step using biological networks into a high-dimensional ML discovery pipeline is a significant advance over purely agnostic searches. Furthermore, tailoring XAI methods specifically to decompose and visualize validated genetic interactions within a causal inference framework moves beyond standard feature importance rankings.

proposals.title: Functional Validation of Blood Pressure-Associated Haplotypes: Integrating Allele-Specific 3D Chromatin Architecture with CRISPR-Based Causal Loop Interrogation
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous non-coding variants associated with blood pressure (BP) and cardiometabolic disease, but establishing their causal mechanisms remains a major challenge. Population-level Hi-C maps provide correlative links between risk loci and putative target genes, but they cannot distinguish causal loops from passive co-localization or assign function to specific risk alleles on a haplotype. This gap limits the translation of genetic risk scores into actionable biological insights for disease prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific chromatin loops, physically connecting non-coding BP risk variants to gene promoters, are necessary for allele-specific gene regulation and that precise disruption of these loops will causally alter target gene expression and relevant cellular phenotypes.
proposals.method: We will first perform high-resolution promoter-focused Capture Hi-C (CHi-C) and haplotype-resolved Hi-C (using parental genome phasing or long-read sequencing) in human vascular endothelial cells (HUVECs) and induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived cardiomyocytes to identify allele-specific chromatin interactions at BP GWAS loci. Candidate causal loops will be prioritized using integrated analyses of allele-specific contact frequency, chromatin accessibility (ATAC-seq), and histone marks. We will then employ CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing to precisely delete or invert the variant-containing loop anchor sequence in isogenic cellular models and use 4C-seq or Micro-C to quantify the resultant changes in 3D architecture. The functional impact will be assessed by measuring allele-specific expression of the putative target gene via RT-qPCR and RNA-seq, and by assaying relevant cellular phenotypes (e.g., nitric oxide signaling, contractility).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct experimental pipeline to move from statistical genetic association to validated causal mechanism for BP variants. It will generate a resource of functionally annotated, allele-resolved chromatin loops in relevant cell types. The findings will refine polygenic risk scores by distinguishing functional from non-functional variants and identify novel regulatory pathways for therapeutic targeting.
proposals.novelty: While population-based 3D maps and CRISPR editing exist, their integration for the allele-resolved, causal dissection of specific chromatin loops—moving beyond correlation to necessity testing in disease-relevant haplotypes—is a novel and critical advance for functional genomics.

proposals.title: A Network Tomography Framework for Inferring Variant-to-Gene Paths in Chromatin Interaction Networks: Application to Blood Pressure GWAS
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of non-coding variants associated with blood pressure (BP) and hypertension risk, but linking these variants to their causal target genes remains a major bottleneck. Existing methods, such as simple proximity or correlation with expression (eQTLs), often fail for distal regulatory elements. While chromatin conformation capture (Hi-C) data provides a physical interaction map, it is sparse, noisy, and population-level inference of variant-gene connections is statistically challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a statistical inference framework analogous to network tomography, applied to population-scale Hi-C and genotype data, can robustly reconstruct the most probable 3D chromatin paths connecting BP-associated variants to gene promoters, outperforming naive adjacency-based methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt algorithms from network tomography, specifically employing a factor graph or expectation-maximization approach to treat Hi-C contact matrices as observed, correlated measurements of a latent 3D interaction network. BP-associated variants from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) will serve as source 'probes'. The method will statistically infer the likelihood of a chromatin path between each variant and candidate gene promoters by jointly modeling contact frequency, distance decay, and topological constraints. Validation will use CRISPR perturbation data (e.g., from ENCODE) and colocalization with cis-eQTLs for prioritized genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational tool for probabilistically linking non-coding GWAS variants to target genes, directly addressing a core need in post-GWAS functional interpretation. It will generate a prioritized set of candidate causal genes for BP loci, providing new biological insights and therapeutic targets for hypertension. The method will be generalizable to other complex traits studied in large cohorts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual translation of network tomography—a field from computer network diagnostics—to the statistical inference of 3D genomic connections, moving beyond static contact maps to a probabilistic model of functional paths influenced by genetic variation.

proposals.title: Integrating Spatial Confounder Models and Family-Based Designs for Causal Inference on Gene-Environment Interaction in Cardiometabolic Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: Gene-environment interaction (GxE) studies are crucial for understanding the etiology of complex cardiometabolic diseases like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease. However, existing observational studies are confounded by spatial autocorrelation in environmental exposures (e.g., air pollution, food environment) and unmeasured population stratification, leading to spurious GxE signals. A robust causal inference framework is needed to move beyond mere association and estimate how local context modifies genetic risk.
proposals.hypothesis: That conditioning on high-resolution spatial models of environmental and genetic population structure, within a family-based causal design, will yield unbiased estimates of direct genetic effects and their modification by local environmental factors, revealing true GxE for cardiometabolic traits.
proposals.method: We will leverage the EPIC-Norfolk cohort's geocoded data and genetic resources. First, we will model environmental exposures (e.g., NO2, green space, fast-food outlet density) using geographically weighted regression and kriging. Concurrently, we will model fine-scale population genetic structure using principal components from genome-wide data interpolated via spatial kriging. These spatial confounder models will then be integrated into a family-based, sibling-comparison causal inference pipeline. This design uses genetic variants as instrumental variables within families to estimate direct effects, with interaction terms between polygenic risk scores and spatially-modeled environments tested for significance, controlling for the spatial confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodological pipeline for robust GxE inference that explicitly accounts for spatial confounding. It will generate new, causally-informed evidence on how specific local environmental factors modify genetic liability for cardiometabolic disease, moving from risk prediction to mechanistic understanding. The framework will be a template for spatial causal inference in other cohorts.
proposals.novelty: The integration of spatial epidemiology techniques (kriging, GWR) directly into a family-based causal inference framework for GxE is novel. It moves beyond simply adjusting for crude area-level covariates or genetic PCs by explicitly modeling the continuous spatial structure of both environmental and genetic confounders as a pre-processing step for causal analysis.

proposals.title: Functional Triage of Rare Genetic Variants in Multiplex Families to Elucidate Metabolic Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: Large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous loci associated with type 2 diabetes and related cardiometabolic traits, but the causal variants and genes, particularly for rare variants with larger effect sizes, remain largely unknown. This 'missing functional mechanism' gap limits the translation of genetic findings into biological insight and clinical utility. Multiplex families with high disease burden are a powerful resource for nominating rare candidate variants, but distinguishing functional drivers from private, neutral polymorphisms remains a major bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating deep sequencing in multiplex families with high-throughput functional screening will efficiently identify rare, non-coding variants with significant regulatory effects on gene expression in disease-relevant cell types, and that these variants will be enriched in pathways central to metabolic disease etiology.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome sequencing in selected multiplex families from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort with extreme metabolic phenotypes to nominate candidate rare variants. Candidate cis-regulatory sequences (e.g., promoters, enhancers) harboring these variants will be synthesized into a plasmid library for a Massively Parallel Reporter Assay (MPRA). This library will be transfected into human pancreatic beta-cell and adipocyte models to quantitatively measure allele-specific regulatory activity. Variants showing significant effects will be validated using CRISPR-Cas9 editing in induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived hepatocytes or beta-like cells, followed by assays of gene expression and cellular metabolism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable pipeline for moving from familial genetic signals to functionally validated mechanisms, directly contributing to the 'functional follow-up' of GWAS loci. It will produce a prioritized list of high-confidence rare regulatory variants impacting metabolic disease risk. The findings will enhance polygenic risk scores by incorporating functional weightings and identify novel targets for mechanistic investigation.
proposals.novelty: While MPRA and family-based sequencing exist separately, their integrated application to triage rare variants from prospective population cohorts like EPIC-Norfolk is novel. This approach moves beyond statistical association to directly test the regulatory consequence of variants in their native genomic context within disease-relevant cell types, a critical step for causal inference.

proposals.title: Multi-Trait Genetic Mega-Analysis of Blood Pressure and Cardiometabolic Phenotypes: Uncovering Shared Pathways and Improving Polygenic Prediction
proposals.motivation: Blood pressure (BP) is a major modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) and shares complex genetic and metabolic underpinnings with other traits like lipids, adiposity, and glycaemic markers. Pre-2026 GWAS for BP, while large, have primarily been single-trait analyses, potentially missing pleiotropic loci and shared biological mechanisms. A multi-trait mega-analysis can leverage genetic correlations to boost power and identify novel pathways, directly informing more integrated risk prediction and prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-trait mega-analysis integrating BP GWAS with genetic data for lipids, body composition, and type 2 diabetes-related traits will identify novel, shared genetic loci and improve the accuracy of polygenic scores for cardiovascular risk prediction beyond single-trait models.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale, multi-cohort meta-analysis of genome-wide summary statistics for systolic BP, diastolic BP, LDL/HDL cholesterol, triglycerides, waist-hip ratio, and HbA1c from consortia like UK Biobank, ICBP, and GIANT (pre-2026 data). We will apply multi-trait analysis of GWAS (MTAG) and genomic structural equation modelling (Genomic SEM) to estimate genetic correlations and identify novel pleiotropic loci. Resulting multi-trait polygenic scores will be tested for association with incident coronary heart disease and type 2 diabetes in the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, adjusting for lifestyle factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a refined map of shared genetic architecture across key cardiometabolic traits, identifying novel biological pathways for intervention. It will generate and validate a novel, integrated multi-trait polygenic risk score, providing a tool for stratifying individuals at higher genetic risk for CVD who may benefit most from targeted lifestyle or pharmacological prevention, as studied in Dr. Wareham's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While multi-trait methods exist, their application in a mega-analysis framework specifically scaling up BP genetics by integrating a broad panel of pre-specified, clinically relevant cardiometabolic traits from major cohorts is novel. This moves beyond genetic correlation studies to actively improve discovery and prediction for complex disease risk.

proposals.title: A Pathway-Informed Bayesian Mendelian Randomization Framework for Robust Causal Inference in Cardiometabolic Disease
proposals.motivation: Mendelian Randomization (MR) is a cornerstone for causal inference in epidemiology, but widespread pleiotropy violates its core assumptions, biasing estimates. Existing methods treat pleiotropy as a nuisance to be corrected, often with simplistic SNP-level classifications, ignoring the underlying biological pathways. This limits the robust application of MR to complex traits like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, where genetic instruments are highly pleiotropic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that classifying pleiotropic effects into distinct, empirically defined pathways (e.g., trait-mediated via adiposity, biological interaction via metabolite levels, or horizontal) and incorporating this classification into a Bayesian MR model will yield more accurate and robust causal estimates for lifestyle and metabolic risk factors on disease outcomes.
proposals.method: We will first develop a data-driven pleiotropy classification system by applying multi-trait GWAS and colocalization analyses (using tools like MTAG and COLOC) to large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank), integrating functional genomics annotations (e.g., from GTEx, ENCODE). This will map SNPs to pathway classes. We will then build a flexible Bayesian MR model where the prior distribution for a SNP's pleiotropic effect is explicitly conditioned on its assigned pathway class, using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for inference. The model will be validated using simulations and applied to investigate the causal effects of diet quality and physical activity (derived from EPIC-Norfolk) on coronary heart disease and type 2 diabetes risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable framework for MR that systematically accounts for pleiotropy, moving from SNP-level correction to pathway-informed modeling. It will deliver more reliable causal estimates for modifiable risk factors in cardiometabolic disease and produce a novel, publicly available resource classifying genetic instruments by pleiotropic pathway.
proposals.novelty: While existing MR methods (e.g., MR-PRESSO, MR-RAPS) adjust for pleiotropy statistically, our approach is novel in its foundational use of empirical multi-omics data to biologically classify pleiotropy *before* model specification, and in explicitly encoding these classes into the prior structure of a Bayesian model.

proposals.title: A Structural Equation Modeling Framework for Mendelian Randomization to Explicitly Model and Test Pleiotropic Pathways
proposals.motivation: Mendelian randomization (MR) is a powerful tool for causal inference in epidemiology, but its validity hinges on the exclusion restriction—the assumption that genetic variants influence the outcome only via the exposure. Horizontal pleiotropy, where a variant affects the outcome through alternative pathways, is a major source of bias and is often treated as a nuisance to be detected and removed. Current methods (e.g., MR-Egger, weighted median) attempt to correct for pleiotropy but do not explicitly model its biological pathway, limiting mechanistic insight and potentially discarding informative genetic signals.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting structural equation modeling (SEM) to the MR context, we hypothesize that explicitly specifying and estimating the pleiotropic pathway of a genetic variant (e.g., a direct path from variant to outcome) alongside the exposure-mediated path will allow for consistent estimation of the exposure-outcome effect even when the exclusion restriction is violated, and provide a formal test of that assumption.
proposals.method: We will develop an SEM-MR framework where a genetic variant (e.g., rs653178 in the SH2B3/ATXN2 locus, known for pleiotropic associations with cardiometabolic traits) is an exogenous variable. We will specify a model with two paths to the outcome: one mediated by a target exposure (e.g., a specific metabolite or adiposity measure) and one direct path representing pleiotropy. Using individual-level data from cohorts like EPIC-Norfolk, we will estimate path coefficients using maximum likelihood. Model fit indices (e.g., CFI, RMSEA) will compare the full model to a restricted model enforcing no direct path, providing a quantitative test of the exclusion restriction. Sensitivity analyses will incorporate measured potential pleiotropic mediators (e.g., education, other metabolites).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, flexible method for MR that moves from merely detecting pleiotropy to explicitly modeling it, yielding more robust causal estimates. It will generate a formal statistical test for the core MR assumption and enhance the biological interpretation of genetic instruments by quantifying pathway-specific effects, directly informing the design and interpretation of studies on disease risk.
proposals.novelty: While SEM is established in social sciences, its application to MR to explicitly model pleiotropy as a causal pathway, rather than as mere noise, is novel. This contrasts with existing MR methods that adjust for pleiotropy statistically without embedding it within a unified causal diagram.

proposals.title: Calibrating Polygenic Risk Scores for Kidney Function Across Ancestries: A Transfer Learning Framework Using Summary Statistics
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) show substantially reduced predictive accuracy and portability in non-European populations, limiting their clinical utility and exacerbating health disparities. This poor transferability stems from differences in allele frequencies, linkage disequilibrium (LD) patterns, and gene-environment interactions across ancestries. Addressing this portability gap is a critical, unresolved challenge in genomic medicine and public health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a PRS derived from a European-ancestry GWAS can be systematically calibrated for a target population using genome-wide summary statistics, analogous to bias-correction methods in climate modeling, thereby significantly improving its predictive accuracy for eGFR.
proposals.method: We will develop a statistical calibration framework that treats a base PRS as a 'global model'. Using GWAS summary statistics from a target population (e.g., from the UK Biobank's non-European cohorts or external consortia), we will employ penalized regression (e.g., LASSO or elastic net) to estimate population-specific adjustment weights for the imported SNP effects. This model will correct for systematic biases in effect sizes, accounting for differences in LD structure via reference panels (e.g., 1000 Genomes). We will validate the calibrated scores against measured eGFR in independent target-population cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, summary-statistic-based method to enhance the cross-ancestry portability of PRS for kidney function, a key risk factor for cardiometabolic disease. It will contribute a novel analytical framework that moves beyond simple re-weighting, directly addressing the bias-correction analogy, and deliver more equitable genetic risk prediction tools.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual borrowing of bias-correction and downscaling techniques from climate science to the problem of PRS calibration, and in its specific focus on using only summary statistics for calibration, which is more feasible than individual-level data sharing across diverse biobanks.

proposals.title: Assessing the Generalizability of Genetic Risk Scores for Kidney Function: A Comparison of SNP Effect Sizes on eGFR Across Diverse Ancestral Populations
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) are increasingly used to identify individuals at higher risk of chronic kidney disease (CKD). However, most PRS are developed and weighted using effect sizes (beta coefficients) derived from large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) conducted predominantly in European-ancestry populations. This raises critical questions about the portability and predictive accuracy of these scores in non-European populations, potentially exacerbating health disparities in CKD risk prediction and prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: The effect sizes (weights) of single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) associated with eGFR, as derived from European-ancestry GWAS, differ significantly when estimated in populations of non-European ancestry, leading to a systematic miscalibration of eGFR genetic risk scores in these populations.
proposals.method: We will utilize summary statistics from large, pre-existing GWAS of eGFR in populations of European, East Asian, South Asian, and African ancestry (e.g., from consortia like CKDGen and UK Biobank). For a core set of independently associated eGFR SNPs, we will extract and compare their ancestry-specific beta coefficients and standard errors. We will formally test for heterogeneity of effects using Cochran's Q statistic and calculate the genetic correlation across ancestries using LD Score regression. Subsequently, we will construct and compare the performance of ancestry-specific versus cross-ancestry PRS in independent cohorts like EPIC-Norfolk, evaluating metrics of variance explained (R²) and risk stratification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative assessment of the portability of eGFR genetic architecture, directly informing the equitable development of PRS for kidney function. It will yield practical guidance on whether and when population-specific weighting is necessary for accurate risk prediction, contributing to more precise and equitable strategies for identifying individuals at risk of CKD across diverse global populations.
proposals.novelty: While the limited portability of PRS is recognized, this study moves beyond demonstrating reduced prediction accuracy to systematically dissect the specific contribution of heterogeneous SNP effect sizes—as opposed to differences in allele frequency or linkage disequilibrium—to this limitation for a key renal trait, eGFR, leveraging Dr. Wareham's expertise in multi-cohort metabolic disease research.

proposals.title: Deconvolving the Genetic Architecture of Coronary Heart Disease: A Network-Based Approach to Isolate Non-Renal Causal Pathways for the SH2B3/ATXN2 Locus (rs653178)
proposals.motivation: The SNP rs653178 at the SH2B3/ATXN2 locus is a well-established genetic risk factor for coronary heart disease (CHD), but its effect is strongly associated with renal function (eGFR), creating a confounded understanding of its primary causal pathways. Disentangling whether its CHD effect is mediated solely through renal impairment or operates via independent biological mechanisms is critical for identifying novel therapeutic targets. Pre-2026 genetic epidemiology lacks robust computational frameworks to systematically silence confounding pathways and reveal independent genetic effects, a challenge analogous to deconvolving polypharmacological drug actions in network pharmacology.
proposals.hypothesis: By computationally conditioning a multi-omics biological network on renal function variables, we will identify and validate distinct, non-renal pathways (e.g., related to immune regulation or vascular inflammation) that mediate the direct effect of the rs653178 locus on CHD risk.
proposals.method: We will construct a comprehensive causal network centered on the rs653178 locus by integrating cis-QTLs (expression, protein, metabolite), protein-protein interactions (STRING, BioGRID), and pathway databases (Reactome, KEGG). Using a graph-based algorithm adapted from network pharmacology (e.g., network propagation with node silencing), we will computationally condition the network on eGFR-associated nodes and edges. The resulting sub-network of 'non-renal' pathways will be prioritized for validation using two-sample Mendelian Randomization and Bayesian colocalization analyses in large-scale biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, EPIC-Norfolk), employing genetic instruments for the implicated molecular traits.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, systems-level map of the independent biological pathways linking the SH2B3/ATXN2 locus to CHD, moving beyond renal confounders. It will deliver a generalizable analytical framework for deconvolving pleiotropic genetic effects, directly informing the prioritization of mechanistic drug targets. The findings will refine polygenic risk scores by clarifying pathway-specific contributions.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting a computational method from network pharmacology—specifically target-disease edge deconvolution—to solve a core problem in genetic epidemiology. It moves beyond standard Mendelian Randomization by using a prior biological network to generate and conditionally test specific pathway hypotheses.

proposals.title: Disentangling Causal Pathways at the rs653178 Locus: A Multi-Trait Colocalization and Mediation Analysis of Coronary Heart Disease and Kidney Function
proposals.motivation: The rs653178 variant at the ATXN2-SH2B3 locus is a well-established pleiotropic signal associated with both coronary heart disease (CHD) and estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR). Pre-2026 evidence suggests shared genetic architecture, but it remains unresolved whether the CHD risk is primarily mediated through impaired kidney function or operates via independent pathways like inflammation or lipid metabolism. Clarifying this is crucial for understanding disease etiology and identifying the most relevant biological targets for intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between rs653178 and CHD is partially, but not wholly, mediated by its effect on eGFR, with a significant residual direct effect operating through alternative biological pathways.
proposals.method: First, we will perform rigorous colocalization analysis (using COLOC and HEIDI-outlier) on large-scale GWAS summary statistics for CHD and eGFR to confirm a shared causal variant. Subsequently, we will apply bi-directional two-sample Mendelian randomization (using inverse-variance weighted and MR-Egger methods) to assess causality between eGFR and CHD. Finally, we will conduct genetic mediation analysis to formally partition the total genetic effect of rs653178 on CHD into components mediated by eGFR and components acting through independent pathways, leveraging genetic instruments for inflammatory and lipid biomarkers.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond association to provide a formal causal quantification of pathway-specific effects at a key pleiotropic locus. It will clarify the primary biological mechanism linking this variant to CHD risk, informing whether therapeutic strategies should target kidney function, immune pathways, or both. The integrated analytical framework will serve as a model for dissecting complex genetic loci.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have noted associations, this proposal integrates state-of-the-art colocalization with formal genetic mediation to quantitatively partition causal effects, a step beyond standard Mendelian randomization which typically tests for a single mediated pathway.

proposals.title: Pathway-specific mediation of coronary heart disease risk by the pleiotropic SNP rs653178: an investigation of metabolite and lifestyle interactions independent of renal function
proposals.motivation: The SNP rs653178 (ATXN2/SH2B3) is a known pleiotropic locus associated with coronary heart disease (CHD), type 2 diabetes, and renal function. Its association with CHD is often attributed to effects on estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), but pre-2026 evidence suggests it may influence risk through other pathways, including inflammation and lipid metabolism. Understanding these independent mechanisms is crucial for developing targeted prevention strategies that move beyond a singular focus on renal function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between rs653178 and incident CHD is partially mediated by specific circulating metabolites and modified by lifestyle factors, and that these pathways operate independently of its effect on eGFR.
proposals.method: We will use data from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, leveraging detailed phenotypic, genetic, and stored serum data. We will first confirm the association of rs653178 with CHD incidence using Cox regression, adjusting for eGFR. We will then perform high-throughput nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) metabolomic profiling to identify metabolites mediating the rs653178-CHD association, using Mendelian Randomization and mediation analysis (e.g., product-of-coefficients method) to test for independence from eGFR. Finally, we will test for interaction between the rs653178 risk allele and lifestyle scores (derived from diet and physical activity) on CHD risk using multiplicative interaction terms.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the specific biological pathways linking rs653178 to CHD, clarifying its pleiotropic mechanism. It will generate a novel, pathway-informed risk score component and identify potential lifestyle modifiers of genetic risk, contributing to more precise, stratified approaches to CHD prevention.
proposals.novelty: While rs653178's link to CHD is known, this study uniquely dissects its mechanism by integrating genetic, high-resolution metabolomic, and detailed lifestyle data to explicitly test for pathways operating independently of renal function, a key unresolved question in pre-2026 cardiometabolic genetics.

proposals.title: Dissecting the causal pathways linking lifestyle factors to cardiometabolic disease: A multi-mediator Mendelian Randomization and sensitivity analysis framework
proposals.motivation: Lifestyle-based risk scores for diseases like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease are well-established, but the specific biological pathways mediating these associations remain poorly understood. Single-mediator Mendelian Randomization (MR) studies are susceptible to bias from horizontal pleiotropy and unmeasured confounding, limiting causal inference. A robust framework is needed to simultaneously test multiple candidate pathways, such as specific metabolite profiles, to map the direct and indirect effects of modifiable exposures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using multivariable MR (MVMR) with genetic instruments for a lifestyle exposure (e.g., diet quality) and multiple molecular mediators (e.g., circulating metabolites) will allow for the robust dissection of causal pathways, and that the mediated effects identified will withstand rigorous sensitivity analyses for pleiotropy and confounding.
proposals.method: We will apply a two-step MVMR framework. First, we will select genetic instruments for a primary exposure (e.g., a dietary pattern score) and for several candidate circulating metabolites from large-scale GWAS. Second, we will perform MVMR analyses to estimate the direct effect of the exposure on disease (e.g., T2D) and the independent effects of the mediators, adjusting for each other. We will implement extensive sensitivity analyses, including the MR-Egger intercept test, Q-statistics for heterogeneity, and leave-one-out analyses, to quantify the robustness of the mediated pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more reliable and nuanced causal map of how lifestyle factors influence cardiometabolic disease risk through specific biological pathways. It will advance methodological rigor in nutritional and lifestyle epidemiology by integrating multi-mediator models with comprehensive sensitivity frameworks. The findings could inform the development of targeted prevention strategies by identifying key modifiable mediators.
proposals.novelty: This integrates the simultaneous testing of multiple biological pathways within an MVMR framework, moving beyond single-mediator models, and couples it with a systematic, pre-specified battery of sensitivity analyses specifically designed to assess the robustness of mediated effects in the presence of pleiotropy.

proposals.title: A Multi-Resolution Causal Concordance Framework to Dissect Shared Genetic Architecture Between Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate and Coronary Heart Disease
proposals.motivation: Chronic kidney disease, assessed via eGFR, is a major independent risk factor for coronary heart disease (CHD), but the underlying causal pathways remain incompletely understood. Standard genetic colocalization methods often fail to distinguish true biological pleiotropy from linkage disequilibrium, limiting insights into shared pathophysiology. A rigorous, high-resolution assessment of shared causal genetic variants is needed to inform targeted prevention and therapeutic strategies for these interconnected cardiometabolic diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of the observed genetic correlation between eGFR and CHD is driven by true pleiotropic loci where the same causal variant influences both traits, and that a multi-resolution framework can robustly identify these loci while distinguishing them from those with multiple independent signals.
proposals.method: We will implement a sequential pipeline using summary statistics from large-scale GWAS. First, we will identify independent genetic signals for each trait using conditional analysis (GCTA-COJO). Second, we will perform high-resolution fine-mapping at each locus using SuSiE to generate 95% credible sets of causal variants. The core analysis will quantify credible set overlap using a novel probabilistic metric that accounts for LD structure and fine-mapping uncertainty, moving beyond binary colocalization (e.g., COLOC). Sensitivity analyses will include Mendelian Randomization to assess causal direction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a nuanced, locus-specific map of shared genetic causality between kidney function and CHD, identifying high-confidence pleiotropic targets for functional follow-up. It will advance methodological rigor in genetic epidemiology by demonstrating a framework for dissecting complex trait correlations, directly informing the development of integrated risk scores and the repurposing of therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond standard colocalization by integrating fine-mapping uncertainty into a probabilistic assessment of shared causal variants, enabling the distinction between single-variant pleiotropy and complex, multi-signal loci—a key limitation in pre-2026 analyses of genetic correlation.

proposals.title: Network Medicine of the Cardiorenal Axis: Integrating Genomics and Proteomics to Identify Shared Pathways and Predictive Biomarkers for Cardiometabolic Disease
proposals.motivation: The kidney-heart axis is central to cardiometabolic disease, with conditions like chronic kidney disease (CKD) and type 2 diabetes (T2D) being major risk factors for cardiovascular disease (CVD). Pre-2026, epidemiological studies have established strong associations, but the underlying shared biological mechanisms remain incompletely characterized. A network-based approach can move beyond single biomarkers to model the complex, systemic interactions driving this comorbidity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a network medicine framework, integrating genomic and proteomic data, will reveal distinct, shared molecular modules and pathways that underpin the cardiorenal axis, and that these network features will improve risk prediction for incident cardiometabolic events beyond traditional clinical and lifestyle scores.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing deeply-phenotyped cohorts (e.g., EPIC-Norfolk) with genetic data, stored plasma, and long-term cardiometabolic outcomes. Using pre-2026 high-throughput proteomic platforms (e.g., Olink, SomaScan), we will generate proteomic profiles. We will construct multi-omic networks using correlation-based and Gaussian graphical models to identify interconnected modules of proteins and genetic variants. Key driver nodes will be identified using network centrality algorithms (e.g., PageRank). These network features will be integrated into novel prediction models, tested against established lifestyle-based scores.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a systems-level map of the cardiorenal axis, identifying novel mechanistic pathways linking kidney and heart disease. It will generate a proof-of-concept for using network-derived, multi-omic signatures to improve early risk stratification for cardiometabolic disease, directly extending Wareham's work on lifestyle-based prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While network medicine has been applied to single organs, its systematic application to the *inter-organ* kidney-heart axis using integrated genomics and proteomics from a large, prospective cohort is novel. The approach moves from studying associations of individual metabolites or proteins to modeling their collective, interactive biology.

proposals.title: Multi-ancestry Mendelian randomization analysis of lifestyle-related metabolites and cardiometabolic disease risk in large-scale biobanks
proposals.motivation: Mendelian randomization (MR) studies using metabolomic data have identified potential causal biomarkers for cardiometabolic diseases, but their generalizability is limited by reliance on predominantly European-ancestry cohorts of modest size. This restricts power for detecting subtle effects and understanding potential ancestry-specific heterogeneity, which is crucial for developing globally relevant risk scores and interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: That scaling MR analyses of lifestyle-associated metabolites (e.g., branched-chain amino acids, lipoprotein subclasses) to larger, multi-ancestry biobanks will reveal novel, robust genetic instruments and causal associations for type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, some of which may show ancestry-specific effects.
proposals.method: We will perform a two-sample MR analysis using genetic variants strongly associated (p < 5e-8) with circulating metabolite levels from large-scale genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics. Exposure data will be derived from the largest available pre-2026 metabolomic GWAS, scaled by meta-analyzing across ancestries where possible. Outcome data will be sourced from vastly larger, multi-ancestry biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, All of Us, Biobank Japan) for type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease. We will apply inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary method, with sensitivity analyses (MR-Egger, weighted median) to assess pleiotropy, and conduct stratified analyses by genetic ancestry.
proposals.contribution: This will substantially increase the power and precision of causal estimates for metabolite-disease pathways. It will provide the first large-scale assessment of the trans-ancestry portability of these causal relationships, informing whether metabolite-based risk prediction models and therapeutic targets can be generalized globally or require ancestry-specific calibration.
proposals.novelty: While MR on metabolites is established, this proposal leverages the recent dramatic scaling of multi-ancestry biobanks to move beyond discovery in European populations, systematically testing for heterogeneity and improving causal inference power for lifestyle-related metabolic pathways in understudied groups.

proposals.title: Mendelian Randomization to Identify Causal Pathways from Lifestyle and Metabolites to Subclinical Coronary Atherosclerosis: A Study Using Coronary Artery Calcium Score as a Continuous Biomarker Outcome
proposals.motivation: Coronary heart disease (CHD) risk prediction traditionally relies on clinical endpoints, which are distal and require long follow-up. Intermediate, quantitative biomarkers like coronary artery calcium (CAC) score provide a continuous, direct measure of subclinical atherosclerosis, offering greater statistical power and mechanistic insight. Understanding the causal influence of modifiable lifestyle factors and their associated metabolite profiles on this early disease stage is crucial for targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetically instrumented lifestyle factors (e.g., diet quality, physical activity) and specific circulating metabolites have causal, independent effects on the degree of subclinical coronary atherosclerosis, as measured by the continuous CAC score.
proposals.method: We will perform two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) analyses using summary-level genetic data. Genetic instruments for lifestyle factors will be derived from large-scale GWAS consortia (e.g., UK Biobank). Instruments for metabolites will be sourced from metabolomics GWAS. The primary outcome will be genetic associations with the continuous, inverse normal transformed CAC score from studies like the UK Biobank and Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA). We will employ inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary analysis, with sensitivity analyses (MR-Egger, weighted median, MR-PRESSO) to assess pleiotropy. Multivariable MR will be used to dissect independent effects of correlated exposures.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond binary CHD risk to identify causal drivers of the underlying disease continuum, providing stronger evidence for intervention targets. It will clarify whether the cardioprotective effects of lifestyle and metabolites are mediated through the attenuation of coronary calcification. The findings will inform the refinement of risk scores and the development of early-stage biomarkers for intervention trials.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been applied to CHD incidence, its application to a continuous, imaging-based measure of subclinical atherosclerosis as the outcome is novel. This approach integrates Wareham's expertise in lifestyle-based prediction with cutting-edge genetic and metabolomic methods to interrogate causal pathways at an earlier, more modifiable disease stage.

proposals.title: Causal inference of lifestyle factors on post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection: a Mendelian randomization study in UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: Observational studies have identified associations between lifestyle factors (e.g., diet, physical activity) and the risk of post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC, or Long COVID), but these are prone to confounding and reverse causation. Establishing causality is critical for developing effective, evidence-based public health and clinical recommendations for PASC prevention and management. Mendelian randomization (MR) offers a powerful method to infer causality using genetic variants as instrumental variables, but it has not been systematically applied to the PASC-lifestyle relationship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetically predicted healthier lifestyle profiles, including higher diet quality and greater physical activity, have a causal protective effect on the risk of developing post-acute sequelae following SARS-CoV-2 infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-sample MR analysis using genetic instruments for lifestyle factors derived from large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) available pre-2026. Instruments will be selected for composite lifestyle scores, diet quality (e.g., based on the planetary health diet index), and physical activity. The outcome will be PASC, defined via linked health records and self-report in SARS-CoV-2 positive participants of the UK Biobank. We will employ inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary analysis, with sensitivity analyses including MR-Egger, weighted median, and MR-PRESSO to assess and correct for pleiotropy.
