Analyses will be stratified by sex and adjusted for genetic principal components.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust causal estimates for the effect of modifiable lifestyle factors on PASC risk, moving beyond observational associations. It will directly inform the development of targeted lifestyle-based prevention strategies. Furthermore, it will demonstrate the utility of MR for investigating the etiology of post-infectious syndromes, a novel application of the method.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been used to study lifestyle and chronic diseases, its application to PASC is novel. The integration of a genetically instrumented planetary health diet score with a PASC outcome bridges Dr. Wareham's expertise in lifestyle-disease associations with the urgent, post-2020 public health challenge of Long COVID.

proposals.title: Distinct Immunometabolic Biomarker Profiles of Post-COVID-19 Condition Subtypes: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Post-COVID-19 Condition (PCC) is a heterogeneous syndrome with proposed clinical subtypes (e.g., cardiopulmonary, fatigue-dominant, neurocognitive), but their underlying biological mechanisms remain poorly characterized. Understanding whether these subtypes are driven by distinct, persistent immunological dysregulation or metabolic disturbances is crucial for developing targeted therapies and moving beyond a one-size-fits-all management approach. This aligns with the researcher's expertise in linking lifestyle, metabolic profiles, and disease risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinically defined PCC subtypes are associated with distinct, measurable profiles of circulating inflammatory cytokines, immune cell phenotypes, and metabolite concentrations, reflecting divergent pathophysiological pathways.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing EPIC-Norfolk cohort with linked primary care and hospital data to identify confirmed PCC cases, applying a validated symptom clustering algorithm to define subtypes. Using stored blood samples from pre-pandemic and post-infection timepoints, we will perform high-throughput profiling: 1) Olink® Target 96 Inflammation panel for cytokines, 2) flow cytometry for immune cell subsets (e.g., T cell exhaustion markers), and 3) untargeted NMR metabolomics. We will use multivariable logistic and linear regression models, adjusted for pre-pandemic cardiometabolic risk factors, to test associations between subtypes and biomarker profiles, and machine learning (e.g., random forest) to identify the most discriminatory biomarkers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, population-based evidence linking PCC clinical phenotypes to specific immunometabolic dysregulation, moving the field from symptom description to mechanism. It will generate a novel, biologically-informed risk score for subtype classification to guide future clinical trials. The findings will also inform whether PCC subtypes share pathophysiological features with chronic diseases like type 2 diabetes, where similar low-grade inflammation is implicated.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have described PCC biomarkers, none have systematically linked pre-existing cohort data, detailed clinical subtyping, and multi-platform ‘omics to dissect mechanistic heterogeneity. The use of pre-pandemic biosamples as a baseline is a unique strength for establishing causality.

proposals.title: Computational phenotyping of cardiometabolic disease trajectories from electronic health records: associations with lifestyle, genetic, and metabolite risk scores
proposals.motivation: Electronic health records (EHRs) offer a rich, real-world data source for studying disease progression, but cardiometabolic conditions like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease are often recorded as static diagnoses, missing critical pre-diagnostic trajectories and phenotypic heterogeneity. Pre-2026, lifestyle-based risk scores and metabolite profiles have shown promise in predicting disease onset, but their longitudinal associations with detailed, data-driven clinical phenotypes derived from routine care are poorly characterized. This limits personalized prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unsupervised computational phenotyping of EHR data will reveal distinct sub-phenotypes and temporal trajectories of pre-cardio-metabolic conditions (PCC), and that these data-driven phenotypes will show differential associations with established lifestyle, genetic, and metabolite-based risk scores.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-step informatics pipeline to a large-scale EHR database (e.g., UK Biobank primary care linkage). First, we will use natural language processing (NLP) and rule-based algorithms to extract structured concepts related to symptoms, medications, and lab values. We will then apply unsupervised machine learning methods, specifically latent class mixed models and sequence analysis, to identify distinct longitudinal phenotypic clusters. Finally, we will use regression models to test associations between these trajectory clusters and pre-specified risk scores for diet, physical activity, polygenic risk, and serum metabolites, adjusting for confounders like education.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate novel, data-driven definitions of cardiometabolic disease progression grounded in real-world clinical practice. It will directly test how well established research-based risk scores correlate with these observed clinical phenotypes, potentially validating or refining them for clinical use. The findings could inform more targeted, phenotype-specific preventive interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used EHRs for prediction, this study uniquely focuses on unsupervised discovery of pre-diagnostic phenotypic trajectories and integrates them with multi-domain risk scores (lifestyle, genetic, metabolite) from cohort studies, a synthesis not commonly performed pre-2026.

proposals.title: Deep Metabolic Phenotyping of Prediabetes Subtypes: A Small-N Study to Elucidate Heterogeneous Pathways to Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including work from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, has established that type 2 diabetes (T2D) risk is heterogeneous, with distinct pathophysiological pathways (e.g., insulin resistance vs. beta-cell dysfunction) often described as 'prediabetes clusters' or 'PCC subtypes'. However, large-scale epidemiological studies lack the granular, multi-omic biomarker data needed to fully characterize the distinct metabolic derangements and environmental triggers underlying each high-risk subtype. This gap limits the development of targeted, precision prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intensive deep phenotyping of individuals from established high-risk prediabetes subtypes will reveal distinct, subtype-specific profiles of circulating metabolites, inflammatory markers, and genetic risk scores, which are differentially associated with lifestyle factors and short-term progression of dysglycemia.
proposals.method: We will recruit a small, intensively characterized cohort (N=60-80) from the existing EPIC-Norfolk or similar cohort, selecting equal numbers of participants from 3-4 pre-defined high-risk prediabetes clusters. Each participant will undergo exhaustive profiling, including fasting and postprandial measures, a panel of ~200 targeted metabolites (e.g., lipids, amino acids, glycolysis intermediates) via mass spectrometry, inflammatory cytokines, and detailed physical activity/sleep monitoring. We will apply partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) to identify metabolite patterns distinguishing subtypes, and use linear mixed models to test associations between subtype-specific metabolite scores, genetic risk scores (for T2D and related traits), and concurrent changes in diet quality and adiposity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide an unprecedented, high-resolution metabolic map of prediabetes heterogeneity, moving beyond associations to mechanistic profiling. It will generate novel hypotheses about subtype-specific pathological pathways and identify candidate biomarkers for monitoring subtype progression or response to targeted lifestyle interventions in future trials.
proposals.novelty: While prediabetes subtyping is established, this study uniquely applies a 'deep phenotyping' approach from a small-N, hypothesis-generating perspective within a well-characterized prospective cohort framework, integrating detailed metabolite data with genetic and granular lifestyle exposures to deconstruct the 'black box' of subtype-specific risk.

proposals.title: Modelling non-linear and interactive influences of genetic and lifestyle factors on the plasma proteome: a machine learning approach in the EPIC-Norfolk cohort
proposals.motivation: Plasma proteins are key biomarkers and mediators of cardiometabolic disease. Current epidemiological models, including polygenic risk scores and lifestyle scores, typically assume linear and additive effects, potentially missing critical non-linear and gene-environment interactions that could improve risk prediction and elucidate biological pathways. This limits the development of more precise, personalised prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the influence of genetic predisposition and modifiable lifestyle factors on circulating protein levels is characterised by significant non-linearities and statistical interactions, and that modelling these complex relationships will substantially improve the prediction of protein levels compared to standard linear additive models.
proposals.method: We will leverage the deeply phenotyped EPIC-Norfolk cohort, integrating data on genetic risk scores (for cardiometabolic traits), detailed lifestyle factors (diet, physical activity, adiposity), and a panel of plasma proteins (e.g., from Olink or SOMAscan platforms). We will employ gradient boosting machines (XGBoost) and feed-forward neural networks to model protein levels, explicitly comparing their performance to standard linear regression. Model interpretation will use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values to identify and quantify key non-linear effects and interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-dimensional assessment of gene-lifestyle interplay on the proteome, moving beyond simple additive associations. It will generate specific, testable hypotheses about biological effect modification and deliver improved predictive models for protein levels, a crucial step towards more dynamic risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined linear associations, this proposal systematically applies and compares advanced, interpretable machine learning methods specifically to relax the linearity and additivity assumptions for the joint effects of genetics and lifestyle on the proteome in a large prospective cohort.

proposals.title: Decomposing the genetic and lifestyle contributions of modifiable risk factors to the plasma proteome: a Mendelian randomization and lifestyle interaction analysis in EPIC-Norfolk
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological models treat factors like BMI as modifiable, but their strong genetic determinants may constrain true modifiability. Understanding the relative genetic versus lifestyle contributions of these factors to the circulating proteome—a key mediator of disease—is crucial for refining intervention targets and personalizing prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The association between putatively modifiable risk factors (e.g., BMI, diet quality) and the plasma proteome is dominated by genetic predisposition rather than environmentally malleable components, limiting the causal pathways available for lifestyle intervention.
proposals.method: Using the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, we will apply two-step Mendelian randomization (2SMR) with genetic instruments for BMI and diet to estimate the genetically-predicted component of proteomic associations. We will compare these to associations from observed lifestyle measures using multivariable-adjusted regression. For proteins with significant signals from both approaches, we will test for interaction between genetic risk scores and lifestyle factors using likelihood ratio tests, implemented in R with packages like TwoSampleMR and METAL.
proposals.contribution: This will quantify the 'immutable core' of modifiable risk factors, providing a more nuanced view of intervention efficacy. It will identify specific proteins for which lifestyle changes could override genetic predisposition, informing targeted public health messaging and biomarker development.
proposals.novelty: While genetics and lifestyle are studied separately, this proposal directly contrasts their contributions to the proteome within a single framework, challenging the assumption of uniform modifiability inherent in traditional risk prediction scores.

proposals.title: Mapping the Metabolomic Architecture of Cardiometabolic Disease Across Diverse Populations and Disease States: A Replication and Extension Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 metabolomic studies, including those by Wareham et al., have successfully mapped metabolite-disease associations, but largely in healthy, predominantly European-origin cohorts. This limits the generalizability of derived risk scores and biological insights, as metabolite profiles and their disease associations may vary significantly by ancestry, pre-existing disease, and lifestyle factors in underrepresented populations. Addressing this gap is critical for equitable precision medicine and for understanding the full spectrum of disease pathophysiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the associations between circulating metabolites and incident cardiometabolic diseases (type 2 diabetes, coronary heart disease) differ in magnitude and direction across ancestrally diverse populations and in individuals with pre-existing metabolic conditions, and that incorporating this heterogeneity will improve the predictive performance and clinical utility of metabolomic risk scores.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing large-scale biobanks with diverse participants (e.g., UK Biobank, All of Us) and targeted recruitment of non-healthy cohorts (e.g., from diabetes clinics). Using pre-2026 high-throughput NMR and LC-MS metabolomic platforms, we will profile a standardized panel of ~250 metabolites. We will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, stratified by ancestry and baseline disease status, to replicate and extend prior association maps. Machine learning approaches (e.g., LASSO regression and random forests) will be used to develop and validate ancestry- and context-specific predictive models, comparing them to existing scores derived from healthy European populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, publicly available map of metabolite-disease associations across major global ancestries and disease states. It will generate validated, population-specific metabolomic risk scores for type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, directly addressing health disparities. The findings will reveal novel biological pathways that may be uniquely relevant to disease progression in underrepresented groups.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have noted ethnic differences in single metabolites, this proposal systematically tests the generalizability of the entire metabolomic 'association map' across diverse populations and disease contexts, a crucial step for translation. It moves beyond discovery in healthy cohorts to active validation in the complex, real-world populations where clinical tools are most needed.

proposals.title: Multi-omic integration of proteomic and metabolomic data to elucidate biological pathways linking modifiable lifestyle factors to incident type 2 diabetes in the EPIC-Norfolk cohort
proposals.motivation: While proteomic and metabolomic data individually provide powerful insights into disease risk, their integration can reveal the functional pathways through which modifiable factors like diet and physical activity influence disease. Pre-2026, most epidemiological studies using omics data have been single-layer, missing the opportunity to map the causal cascade from exposure to intermediate molecular phenotypes to clinical endpoint. This limits the development of mechanistic, multi-omic risk scores and targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating proteomic and metabolomic data will identify novel, robust multi-omic pathways that mediate the associations between modifiable lifestyle factors (specifically diet quality and physical activity) and incident type 2 diabetes, beyond what is possible with either layer alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing pre-2026 data from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort, including lifestyle assessments, ~3,000 plasma proteins (Olink Explore), and ~200 serum metabolites (Nightingale NMR platform). We will first use multivariable-adjusted linear regression to identify proteins and metabolites associated with a validated lifestyle score. Subsequently, we will apply a multi-omic network integration approach, such as DIABLO (Data Integration Analysis for Biomarker discovery using Latent cOmponents) from the mixOmics R package, to identify correlated multi-omic modules predictive of T2D incidence. Mediation analysis will test if these modules statistically mediate the lifestyle-T2D association.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, pathway-informed multi-omic signature of lifestyle-related diabetes risk, moving beyond simple association lists. It will provide mechanistic insight into how lifestyle factors converge on specific biological processes, offering potential targets for prevention. The findings will directly inform the development of next-generation, biologically-grounded prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While Wareham's prior work has examined lifestyle, metabolites, and disease, this proposal uniquely integrates the emerging proteomic map with metabolomic data in a large prospective cohort to explicitly model mediation, moving from association to mechanistic pathway discovery.

proposals.title: Global Assessment of the Dimensionality of Adiposity: A Multi-Cohort Analysis of the Correlation and Shared Metabolic Pathways of Body Mass Index and Waist-to-Height Ratio
proposals.motivation: Body mass index (BMI) and waist-to-height ratio (WHtR) are foundational, distinct metrics in epidemiological research, assumed to capture general adiposity and central/visceral fat, respectively. This distinction underpins risk scores and etiological models for cardiometabolic disease. However, if their correlation is universally high across diverse populations, it challenges the assumption of distinct physiological pathways, potentially simplifying risk assessment and clarifying the core adiposity construct.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that BMI and WHtR are so highly correlated across global populations that they represent a single latent construct of overall adiposity, and that this shared construct, rather than metric-specific variance, drives their associations with incident type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease.
proposals.method: We will harmonize individual-level data from major international cohorts (e.g., EPIC, INTERHEART, UK Biobank). Using confirmatory factor analysis, we will test a single-factor versus a two-factor model for BMI and WHtR across sex, age, and ancestral groups. We will then employ Mendelian Randomization (using pre-2026 genetic instruments for adiposity traits) and metabolite profiling (via pre-2026 NMR platforms) to test if both metrics associate with the same genetic and circulating metabolite signatures, indicative of shared upstream biology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, global test of adiposity metric dimensionality, directly informing the construction of parsimonious risk prediction models. It will clarify whether advocating for both measures in clinical and public health guidelines is necessary or redundant. The integration of genetic and metabolomic data will move the debate from correlation to shared causal biology.
proposals.novelty: While correlations have been reported in single cohorts, a formal, global test of their latent dimensionality using causal and molecular methods is lacking. This directly challenges a core, often untested, assumption in nutritional and chronic disease epidemiology.

proposals.title: Development and validation of an ensemble machine learning model integrating anthropometric and regional data for personalised hypertension risk classification in UK adults
proposals.motivation: Hypertension is a major modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes, yet traditional risk scores often rely on limited clinical measures. Pre-2026 evidence suggests body mass index (BMI) and waist-to-height ratio (WHtR) capture distinct aspects of adiposity-related risk, while geographic and socioeconomic factors significantly influence disease incidence. A more integrated, personalised prediction tool is needed for targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble machine learning model that integrates BMI, WHtR, and regional socioeconomic/environmental data will provide superior discrimination and calibration for estimating individual 10-year hypertension probability compared to models using single anthropometric measures or traditional logistic regression.
proposals.method: We will utilise data from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort and other UK Biobank-linked regional datasets. Candidate predictors will include BMI, WHtR, age, sex, plus area-level indices of deprivation, green space, and food environment. We will train and compare a stacked ensemble model, combining base learners (e.g., Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machines, and a regularised logistic regression) via a meta-learner (logistic regression). Hyperparameter tuning will use nested cross-validation to optimise the log-loss and AUC-ROC.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available risk tool that moves beyond single metrics to a multi-factorial, person-specific probability estimate. It will provide empirical evidence on the relative and combined predictive value of central adiposity (WHtR) and regional determinants for hypertension, informing both clinical assessment and public health resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Wareham's prior work developed lifestyle-based models, this proposal innovates by applying a stacked ensemble ML architecture specifically to integrate dual anthropometric phenotypes with geospatial data for hypertension risk, a synthesis not yet fully explored in pre-2026 prospective cohorts.

proposals.title: Comparative clinical and population health utility of BMI and waist-to-height ratio for cardiometabolic disease risk: a dual analysis in the EPIC-Norfolk cohort
proposals.motivation: Body mass index (BMI) and waist-to-height ratio (WHtR) are widely used to assess adiposity-related risk, but their distinct utilities are conflated. For clinical practice, a measure must accurately diagnose high-risk individuals, while for public health, it must effectively stratify population risk to guide preventive resource allocation. A formal, separate evaluation of these two distinct utilities for BMI and WHtR is needed to inform appropriate use in different contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that WHtR will demonstrate superior clinical diagnostic accuracy for identifying individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, while BMI will show greater utility for population-level risk stratification and preventive targeting due to its simpler measurement and stronger association with population-attributable risk.
proposals.method: Using the prospective EPIC-Norfolk cohort, we will conduct a dual analysis. For clinical utility, we will compare the diagnostic accuracy of BMI and WHtR for incident cardiometabolic disease using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, net reclassification improvement (NRI), and decision curve analysis. For population health utility, we will model population-attributable risk fractions and use Gini coefficients to compare the efficiency of risk stratification, simulating the number needed to screen under different targeting strategies based on each measure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, dual-framework evidence base for guideline bodies, clarifying whether WHtR should replace BMI in clinical diagnostics while affirming BMI's potential ongoing value for public health strategy. It will advance methodological approaches for evaluating health metrics by formally separating individual-level diagnostic performance from population-level preventive efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies compare the predictive strength of adiposity measures, none have formally and separately evaluated their distinct clinical diagnostic versus population preventive utilities within the same cohort, a critical distinction for translating epidemiological associations into practice.

proposals.title: Causal inference of meat consumption on cardiometabolic disease risk and related metabolites: A two-sample Mendelian randomization study
proposals.motivation: Observational studies consistently link higher meat intake, particularly red and processed meat, with increased risks of type 2 diabetes (T2D) and coronary heart disease (CHD). However, these associations are susceptible to confounding and reverse causality. Establishing a causal relationship is crucial for refining dietary guidelines and public health interventions. Mendelian randomization (MR) offers a method to strengthen causal inference by using genetic variants as instrumental variables for exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a genetically predicted higher preference and intake of meat causally increases the risk of T2D and CHD and alters specific circulating metabolite profiles associated with these diseases.
proposals.method: We will perform a two-sample MR analysis. First, we will identify genetic instruments (independent SNPs) robustly associated with meat intake from the largest available pre-2026 genome-wide association study (GWAS) on dietary traits (e.g., UK Biobank). We will then obtain genetic association estimates for outcomes from large-scale consortia GWAS for T2D, CHD, and a panel of metabolites. The primary analysis will use an inverse-variance weighted (IVW) method, supplemented by sensitivity analyses (MR-Egger, weighted median, MR-PRESSO) to assess and correct for pleiotropy. Analyses will be stratified by meat type (red, processed, poultry) where possible.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the most robust genetic evidence to date on the causal role of meat consumption in cardiometabolic disease etiology. It will clarify whether observed associations are causal or driven by lifestyle confounding. Furthermore, by integrating metabolite data, it may elucidate biological pathways (e.g., related to iron metabolism, gut microbiome metabolites, or lipid profiles) mediating any observed causal effects.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been applied to broad dietary patterns, using genetic variants specifically associated with meat preference as instruments for detailed meat subtypes is novel. Integrating disease endpoints with metabolomic data in a single MR framework will move beyond risk estimation to mechanistic insight.

proposals.title: Interdependence of Genetic Effects on the Plasma Proteome: A Test of Effect Modification by Sex Hormones in the EPIC-Norfolk Cohort
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores and Mendelian randomization studies typically assume genetic variants act independently on traits. However, this assumption may be violated for complex molecular phenotypes like proteins, where genetic effects could be interdependent and modulated by hormonal context. Testing this is crucial for improving the biological accuracy of genetic models for disease risk, particularly for conditions with known sex disparities like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease.
proposals.hypothesis: The effects of genetic variants on plasma protein levels are not independent; instead, they interact epistatically, and these interactions are significantly modulated by circulating levels of sex hormones (e.g., testosterone, estradiol).
proposals.method: We will use pre-2026 genetic, proteomic (Olink or SomaScan panels), and serum hormone data from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort. First, we will conduct genome-wide protein quantitative trait locus (pQTL) mapping. For proteins with multiple independent pQTLs, we will test for statistical epistasis using regression models with interaction terms, adjusting for age, BMI, and technical covariates. We will then stratify analyses by sex and test for effect modification by hormone levels using multiplicative interaction terms and stratified Mendelian randomization, employing two-stage least squares models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on the pervasiveness of interdependent genetic effects on the proteome, moving beyond the standard additive model. It will elucidate a specific hormonal mechanism (sex steroids) that may explain sex differences in genetic associations with protein levels and downstream cardiometabolic disease risk.
proposals.novelty: While gene-environment interactions are studied, directly testing the violation of genetic effect independence for proteins and formally modeling sex hormones as modulators of genetic epistasis is a novel integration of genetic epidemiology and endocrinology.

proposals.title: Ancestry-specific sex-differential protein quantitative trait loci (sd-pQTLs) and their association with cardiometabolic disease risk: a multi-cohort analysis
proposals.motivation: Genetic regulation of plasma proteins, including sex-differential regulation (sd-pQTLs), is a key mechanism linking genetic risk to cardiometabolic diseases. However, large-scale pQTL studies have been predominantly conducted in European ancestry populations, limiting the generalizability of findings and hindering the development of equitable genetic risk scores. Investigating sd-pQTLs in diverse ancestries is critical to understand population-specific disease biology and address health disparities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of sd-pQTLs are ancestry-specific or show divergent effect sizes between sexes across ancestries, and that these population-specific regulatory variants contribute to differences in cardiometabolic disease risk profiles.
proposals.method: We will perform sex-stratified and combined pQTL mapping in large-scale proteomic datasets from cohorts of non-European ancestries (e.g., African, South Asian, East Asian) available through consortia like the Population Architecture using Genomics and Epidemiology (PAGE) study. Using high-throughput platforms (e.g., SomaScan, Olink), we will test for sd-pQTLs via genotype-by-sex interaction models. We will compare findings to established European sd-pQTLs and assess their association with incident type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease using Mendelian Randomization and polygenic score analyses within the EPIC and other prospective cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first large-scale catalog of sd-pQTLs in multiple non-European ancestries, directly addressing a major gap in genomic medicine. It will provide novel insights into the genetic architecture of the plasma proteome across populations and inform the development of more equitable genetic prediction models for cardiometabolic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While sd-pQTLs have been identified in Europeans, their existence and disease relevance in diverse populations is largely unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates population genetics, proteomics, and prospective disease epidemiology to move beyond cataloging to understanding functional consequences of ancestry-specific regulation.

proposals.title: Sex-differential protein quantitative trait loci (sd-pQTLs) in disease pathogenesis: A Mendelian randomization study in type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease
proposals.motivation: Sex-differential pQTLs (sd-pQTLs) have been identified in population-based proteogenomic studies, revealing genetic variants with sex-specific effects on plasma protein levels. However, it is unknown whether these sd-pQTLs exert the same or altered effects on protein levels within established disease states, such as type 2 diabetes (T2D) or coronary heart disease (CHD). Understanding this is crucial, as disease-associated metabolic and inflammatory milieus may modify genetic effects, potentially explaining sex disparities in disease risk and progression observed in epidemiological studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect sizes and directions of known sd-pQTLs on their target protein levels are significantly modified in individuals with T2D and CHD compared to disease-free controls, and that these modified effects contribute to the sex-specific associations between protein biomarkers and disease outcomes.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing pre-2026 data from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort and UK Biobank, integrating genetic, proteomic (Olink or SOMAscan panels), and deep phenotyping data. For known sd-pQTLs from prior studies, we will perform stratified two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) analyses, comparing the protein level associations of the genetic instruments in disease sub-cohorts versus healthy controls. We will use inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary method, with MR-Egger and weighted median as sensitivity analyses. Statistical interaction terms (sd-pQTL*sex*disease_status) will be tested in multivariable linear regression models to formally assess effect modification.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond cataloguing sd-pQTLs in the general population to functionally testing them in specific disease contexts. It will generate novel insights into the pathophysiology of T2D and CHD by identifying proteins whose genetic regulation is altered by disease state in a sex-specific manner. The findings will inform the development of more precise, sex-stratified genetic risk scores and highlight potential therapeutic targets where intervention effects may differ by sex.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Wareham and others has established lifestyle and genetic risk scores for disease, and population sd-pQTLs are known, the explicit testing of sd-pQTL stability across disease states is novel. This bridges the gap between genetic epidemiology and mechanistic disease biology by considering the disease environment as a modifier of fundamental genetic effects.

proposals.title: Longitudinal assessment of sex-differential protein quantitative trait loci (sd-pQTLs) across the adult lifespan and their association with cardiometabolic disease risk
proposals.motivation: Sex differences in cardiometabolic disease incidence are well-documented but incompletely understood. Pre-2026 studies have identified sex-differential genetic effects on circulating proteins (sd-pQTLs), which may mediate these disparities. However, it is unknown whether these genetic effects are static or change dynamically with age and hormonal transitions, a critical gap for understanding disease risk trajectories and developing sex-specific prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect sizes of sd-pQTLs on plasma protein levels are not fixed but vary longitudinally across the adult lifespan, with significant inflection points associated with major hormonal transitions (e.g., menopause), and that these dynamic genetic effects contribute to age- and sex-specific profiles of cardiometabolic disease risk.
proposals.method: We will leverage the deeply phenotyped EPIC-Norfolk cohort, with serial blood samples and health data, applying a longitudinal mixed-effects modelling framework. Using pre-existing and newly generated Olink proteomic data (Target 96/384 panels), we will first replicate established sd-pQTLs. We will then model protein levels as a function of genotype, age, sex, and their interactions, using spline-based terms to identify non-linear age effects. Mendelian Randomization analyses will assess the causal role of proteins with dynamic sd-pQTL effects on incident type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, stratified by sex and age window.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of how sex-differential genetic regulation of the proteome evolves with age, moving beyond static cross-sectional snapshots. It will identify specific proteins and life stages where genetic risk diverges between sexes, offering novel mechanistic insights into sex-biased disease etiology. The findings will inform more precise, temporally-aware risk prediction scores and highlight potential targets for sex- and age-specific therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified sd-pQTLs, this proposal uniquely investigates their *dynamics* over time within individuals, directly testing the stability of genetic effects. It integrates longitudinal proteomics, genetic data, and detailed lifestyle phenotyping to model how genetic risk manifests differently across the lifespan in men and women.

proposals.title: Integrating Sex-Biased Tissue Expression with Plasma Protein Quantitative Trait Loci to Refine Cardiometabolic Risk Prediction
proposals.motivation: Plasma proteomic quantitative trait loci (pQTLs) are powerful for identifying protein-disease associations, but they often lack tissue-specific context. Pre-2026 studies show that many proteins have sex-biased expression in key metabolic tissues (e.g., liver, adipose, muscle), which may contribute to sex differences in cardiometabolic disease risk. A systematic integration of these data layers is needed to move beyond association to mechanistic insight and more precise risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that plasma pQTLs for proteins with strong sex-biased expression in metabolically relevant tissues will show sex-specific associations with incident type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease, and that incorporating this tissue-specific expression information will improve the performance of lifestyle-based prediction models.
proposals.method: We will use pre-2026 pQTL data from large-scale plasma proteomic studies (e.g., deCODE, Fenland) and sex-stratified tissue expression data from GTEx v8. Proteins will be prioritized based on significant sex-by-tissue interaction terms in expression. For these proteins, we will test for sex-specific associations with disease outcomes in the EPIC-Norfolk cohort using Cox regression, adjusting for established lifestyle risk factors. We will then develop and compare novel prediction scores, integrating pQTL-informed, sex-stratified protein risk scores with traditional lifestyle and metabolite data using penalized regression (LASSO) and assess improvement in C-statistics and net reclassification.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel, biologically informed framework for interpreting plasma pQTLs through a tissue-specific lens. It will produce sex-stratified protein risk scores that can be integrated into existing lifestyle-based models, potentially improving the prediction of cardiometabolic disease and offering insights into underlying sex-specific etiological pathways.
proposals.novelty: While pQTL and tissue expression studies exist separately, their integration to explicitly model sex-biased tissue origin as a modifier of plasma protein-disease associations is novel. This moves from cataloguing associations to generating testable biological hypotheses about disease mechanisms rooted in specific tissues.

proposals.title: Dissecting Sex-Biased Disease Risk: A Causal Mediation Analysis of Genetic Effects Through the Plasma Proteome in the EPIC-Norfolk Cohort
proposals.motivation: Sex differences in the incidence of cardiometabolic diseases like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease are well-documented but mechanistically poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including from EPIC-Norfolk, has identified numerous genetic associations and sex-specific risk scores, yet the causal pathways—particularly the mediating role of circulating proteins—remain largely unmapped. Applying formal causal frameworks is essential to move beyond observational associations and identify actionable biological intermediaries for targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of the genetic risk for sex-biased cardiometabolic diseases is mediated by sex-dimorphic plasma protein levels, and that formal mediation analysis will identify specific proteins that causally explain these disparities.
proposals.method: We will leverage the EPIC-Norfolk cohort's genetic, proteomic (e.g., Olink or SOMAscan panels), and deep phenotyping data. Using a two-step Mendelian Randomization (MR) framework within a causal mediation analysis, we will first estimate the total effect of genetic risk scores (for disease and sex-biased traits) on disease incidence. We will then decompose this effect into direct (genetic->disease) and indirect (genetic->protein->disease) pathways, using protein quantitative trait loci (pQTLs) as instrumental variables. Sensitivity analyses (e.g., for unmeasured confounding) will be conducted using established methods like the MR-Egger and weighted median estimators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal causal dissection of sex-biased disease etiology, moving from associative risk scores to mechanistic pathways. It will generate a prioritized list of protein mediators that could serve as novel biomarkers or therapeutic targets for precision prevention strategies tailored to sex-specific risk. The project will also demonstrate the utility of integrating social science causal methods (mediation) with high-dimensional molecular epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While mediation is common in epidemiology, the application of a formal, MR-based causal mediation framework to dissect sex differences through the proteome is novel. This approach directly addresses the limitation of confounding in traditional observational mediation analyses within a large, well-phenotyped prospective cohort like EPIC-Norfolk.

proposals.title: Sex-differential molecular pathways in cardiometabolic disease: A multi-omic integration of pQTLs, metabolomics, transcriptomics, and methylomics in the EPIC-Norfolk cohort
proposals.motivation: Sex differences in cardiometabolic disease risk are well-documented but mechanistically poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies, including those by Wareham, have established separate associations for genetic (pQTLs), metabolic, and epigenetic markers with disease, but an integrated, sex-stratified analysis is lacking. Such integration is crucial to move beyond observational associations and identify causal, sex-specific biological pathways for targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating sex-stratified pQTL data with metabolomic, transcriptomic, and methylomic profiles will reveal distinct, interconnected molecular networks that mediate sex-differential risk for type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing deep phenotyping from the EPIC-Norfolk cohort. Sex-stratified genome-wide association studies (GWAS) will identify pQTLs for circulating proteins. Using Mendelian Randomization (Two-Sample MR, MR-PRESSO for pleiotropy) and multi-omic network analysis (DIABLO or MOFA+), we will model causal relationships and correlations between pQTL-influenced proteins, metabolites (from NMR/metabolon platforms), blood transcriptomes, and methylation profiles (EPIC arrays). Models will be tested for prediction of incident disease using Cox regression.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first sex-stratified, causal multi-omic map of cardiometabolic disease pathways in a large prospective cohort. It will generate novel, sex-specific biomarker panels and etiological insights, moving the field from single-omic risk scores to integrated pathway-based understanding. Findings will inform the development of sex-specific prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omic studies existed pre-2026, none have systematically integrated pQTLs as causal anchors with three other omic layers while explicitly modeling sex-differential effects across the entire analytical pipeline in a well-characterized cohort like EPIC-Norfolk.

proposals.title: Leveraging Sex-Differential Plasma Proteomics to Prioritize Genetic Variants for Mendelian Disorder Discovery: A Mendelian Randomization and Colocalization Framework
proposals.motivation: Discovering causal genetic variants for rare Mendelian disorders remains challenging, especially for variants with subtle phenotypic effects. Large-scale proteomic studies, such as those from the UK Biobank Pharma Proteomics Project, have identified hundreds of proteins with significant sex differences in circulating levels. These proteins represent high-priority candidates for harboring rare, penetrant variants with sex-biased disease expression, but a systematic framework to use this signal for genetic discovery is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Proteins exhibiting significant sex differences in plasma levels are enriched for nearby rare protein-altering genetic variants that cause Mendelian disorders with sex-biased prevalence or presentation. These variants can be efficiently prioritized by integrating proteomic sex-difference signals with exome or genome sequencing data.
proposals.method: We will first identify proteins with robust sex-differential abundance (p<1e-6) in large pre-2025 proteomic datasets (e.g., UK Biobank, INTERVAL). For each protein's encoding gene, we will perform sex-stratified cis-pQTL mapping. Using two-sample Mendelian randomization (inverse-variance weighted) with sex-specific genetic instruments, we will test for causal effects of protein levels on sex-biased disease traits. Subsequently, we will apply Bayesian colocalization (e.g., COLOC) to identify shared causal variants between sex-differential pQTL signals and rare variant associations from sequencing studies of undiagnosed Mendelian disease cohorts (e.g., Genomics England, 100,000 Genomes Project).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, biologically informed prioritization framework to accelerate the discovery of rare disease genes, particularly for disorders with unexplained sex bias. It will demonstrate the utility of population-scale proteomics as a discovery tool for Mendelian genetics and provide a list of high-priority candidate genes and variants for functional validation.
proposals.novelty: While proteomics is used to interpret genome-wide association study (GWAS) signals, its systematic application as a primary filter to guide rare variant discovery in Mendelian disease is novel. The explicit focus on sex-differential protein levels to uncover variants with sex-biased effects represents a distinct and untapped strategy.

ideas.title: Scale up to multi-trait mega-analysis
ideas.description: Integrate BP GWAS with other cardiovascular traits in a much larger, multi-cohort meta-analysis.
ideas.sourceTitle: Genome-wide association analysis identifies novel blood pressure loci and offers biological insights into cardiovascular risk

ideas.title: Longitudinal Modeling of Intra-Individual Genetic Effect Dynamics for Complex Traits
ideas.description: This study tests the hypothesis that the phenotypic expression of genetic variants for complex traits changes within an individual over their lifespan. It employs a longitudinal, repeated-measures framework using deep electronic health record and biomarker data from large, prospective cohorts to track individuals over time. The analysis specifically models interaction effects between an individual's polygenic score and their aging process, as well as with cumulative metrics of environmental exposure (e.g., pollution dose, dietary patterns, medication history) constructed from their personal timeline. This within-subject design inherently controls for cohort effects and, when paired with appropriate censoring models, mitigates survivorship bias, providing a robust test for dynamic genetic effects.

ideas.title: Disentangle causal pathways for rs653178 using multi-trait colocalization and mediation
ideas.description: This study integrates detailed colocalization analysis with pathway-specific mediation to dissect the genetic architecture of the rs653178 locus. First, rigorous colocalization (e.g., using COLOC or HEIDI) will be applied to large-scale GWAS summary statistics for CHD and eGFR to determine if they share a single causal variant. Subsequently, for loci with evidence of shared causality, bi-directional Mendelian randomization and mediation analysis will be used to partition the total effect of rs653178 on CHD into components mediated by eGFR and components operating through independent biological pathways, such as inflammation or lipid metabolism. This approach moves beyond association to formally test causal relationships and quantify pathway contributions, resolving whether the pleiotropy is mediated or direct.

ideas.title: Relax assumption of linear additive influences
ideas.description: Model non-linear and interactive effects between genetic and modifiable factors on plasma protein levels.
ideas.sourceTitle: Mapping biological influences on the human plasma proteome beyond the genome

ideas.title: Use machine learning for personalized risk classification
ideas.description: Apply ensemble models to integrate BMI, WHtR, and regional data for individual hypertension probability estimation.
ideas.sourceTitle: General and abdominal adiposity and hypertension in eight world regions: a pooled analysis of 837 population-based studies with 7·5 million participants

ideas.title: A constrained, explainable AI framework for discovering and validating polygenic interactions in blood pressure
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-stage, hypothesis-guided machine learning system to discover and validate polygenic interactions. First, non-linear models (e.g., gradient boosting, neural networks) are used to screen for high-order interaction candidates within large-scale genomic and environmental data. Second, these candidates are rigorously filtered using external biological knowledge (e.g., protein-protein interaction networks, co-expression pathways) and statistical genetics methods to assess robustness and potential causality, mitigating false discoveries. Finally, explainable AI techniques are tailored to the genetic context to produce interpretable, biologically-plausible models of gene-gene and gene-environment interplay, enabling both novel discovery and mechanistic validation.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Pharmacology's Target-Disease Edge Deconvolution to Map rs653178's Non-Renal CHD Pathways
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from network pharmacology's approach to deconvolving polypharmacological drug effects, this study proposes to map the pleiotropic effects of SNP rs653178 on CHD risk independent of eGFR. In drug discovery, when a compound's therapeutic effect is confounded by a primary side-effect target, researchers use multi-layered network analysis to silence the confounding pathways and reveal independent mechanisms. We will adapt this solution by constructing a comprehensive causal biological network around the rs653178 locus, integrating QTL data, protein interactions, and pathway databases. By computationally conditioning the network on eGFR and renal function variables, we will isolate and subsequently validate (e.g., via Mendelian Randomization and colocalization in biobanks) the distinct, non-renal biological pathways—such as those involving immune cell function or vascular inflammation—that mediate this genetic variant's direct effect on CHD pathogenesis.

ideas.title: A General Framework for Pathway-Classified Pleiotropy in Mendelian Randomization
ideas.description: This research develops a generalized Mendelian Randomization (MR) method that moves beyond modeling a single SNP's pleiotropy. It first establishes an empirical, data-driven classification system for pleiotropic pathways (e.g., trait-mediated, biological interaction, horizontal) using multi-trait GWAS and functional genomics data. This classification then informs a flexible Bayesian MR model where the prior distributions for pleiotropic effects are explicitly conditioned on the assigned pathway type. The resulting framework systematically relaxes the exclusion restriction for a wide array of genetic instruments, substantially improving robustness and generalizability compared to single-SNP approaches.

ideas.title: A Precision Prevention Trial for Hypertension: Adapting the 'Caries Management by Risk Assessment' Model
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from preventive dentistry—the Caries Management by Risk Assessment (CAMBRA) protocol—to the problem of preventing hypertension. CAMBRA successfully uses a risk-stratified, bundled intervention approach to prevent dental caries in high-risk patients. We propose a 'Hypertension Management by Genetic Risk Assessment' (HM-GRA) clinical trial. Young adults will be stratified by genetic risk for hypertension, and those in the highest risk tier will be randomized to receive either a intensive, multi-modal lifestyle intervention bundle (tailored to their risk profile) or standard preventive advice. The trial will test whether this targeted, early, and combined intervention can alter the long-term blood pressure trajectory and prevent or delay the onset of clinical hypertension in this genetically susceptible population.

ideas.title: Robust causal pathway dissection using multi-mediator MVMR and sensitivity frameworks
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond single-mediator models to dissect pleiotropy by simultaneously testing multiple candidate biological pathways. It uses multivariable Mendelian Randomization with genetic instruments for both the exposure and several molecular mediators to reduce bias from unmeasured confounding. The system rigorously quantifies the robustness of identified mediated effects through extensive sensitivity analyses. This integrated design provides a more reliable and nuanced map of the direct and indirect effects of genetic variants on complex traits.

ideas.title: Challenge heritability as primary risk factor
ideas.description: Investigate if non-heritable factors explain more blood pressure variance in certain environmental contexts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Genome-wide association analysis identifies novel blood pressure loci and offers biological insights into cardiovascular risk

ideas.title: Integrated Genetic, Experimental, and Real-World Evidence Pipeline for Drug Repurposing
ideas.description: This adversarially improved system creates a robust pipeline for drug repurposing that moves beyond genetic association alone. It begins with Mendelian Randomization to generate causal, genetically-supported hypotheses linking drug targets to new diseases. These candidate pairs are then subjected to direct experimental validation in relevant cellular models to confirm the drug's mechanistic effect on the hypothesized pathway. Finally, the pipeline conducts rigorous analyses of real-world clinical data to assess preliminary evidence for efficacy and safety in human populations, thereby de-risking candidates for subsequent clinical trials.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Reverse Epigenetics' Analogy from Chronic Stress to Hypertension
ideas.description: This idea inverts the causal pathway assumption by exploring if chronic blood pressure elevation causally alters genome regulation, not just vice-versa. The analogy comes from Psychoneuroimmunology/Stress Biology, where the solution to a similar problem was demonstrating that chronic psychological stress itself causes lasting epigenetic modifications (e.g., DNA methylation changes) that alter gene expression and physiological function, creating a biological memory of the exposure. Adapted to hypertension research, this means treating chronic elevated arterial pressure as a persistent biophysical driver. The research would test if hypertension directly induces stable epigenetic marks in relevant tissues (vascular, renal, cardiac), aiming to identify a 'pressure epigenome'—regulatory changes caused by the hypertensive state that then perpetuate the disease, thereby reframing hypertension as both a cause and consequence of altered genome regulation.

ideas.title: A Causal Inference Framework for Genotype-Environment Interaction Using Spatial Confounder Modeling
ideas.description: This research system first uses spatial epidemiology techniques (e.g., kriging, geographically weighted regression) to create high-resolution models of environmental exposures and population genetic structure as potential confounders. It then embeds genetic association testing within a causal inference pipeline that conditions on these spatial models, employing family-based or instrumental variable designs to estimate direct genetic effects and their modification by local environment. This approach formally tests for Gene-Environment Interaction (GxE) while controlling for spatial autocorrelation and population stratification, moving beyond correlation to robust, causally-informed inference about how local context shapes genetic effects on complex traits like blood pressure.

ideas.title: Apply network medicine to kidney-heart axis

ideas.title: Challenge that modifiable factors are truly modifiable

ideas.title: Testing genetic effect dynamics using longitudinal cohort analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that genetic effects on blood pressure (BP) are static over the lifespan by adapting a solution from ecology. In ecology, the question of whether species interaction strengths change over time or with environmental conditions is addressed using longitudinal monitoring data and time-varying coefficient state-space models, which separate process dynamics from observation error. Adapted to genetic epidemiology, this involves treating the effect sizes of BP-associated genetic loci as latent variables that can change as functions of age or cumulative environmental exposures (e.g., cumulative smoking, long-term pollution exposure). Using rich longitudinal cohort data with repeated BP measurements and genotyping, a Bayesian state-space modeling framework would be employed to estimate these dynamic genetic effects, explicitly testing for and characterizing any temporal or exposure-dependent changes while rigorously accounting for measurement error and time-varying confounders.

ideas.title: Cell-type-resolved causal network inference from multi-omics data
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond bulk tissue integration by first computationally deconvolving epigenomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic data into cell-type-specific estimates. It then integrates these resolved profiles with GWAS variants, applying causal inference models like Mendelian Randomization across omics layers but strictly within matched cell types. The final output is a set of cell-type-specific causal networks linking genetic variants to molecular phenotypes and disease risk, dramatically improving biological interpretability and identifying precise cellular contexts for disease mechanisms.

ideas.title: Calibrate and Validate Genetic Models Across Diverse Populations Using Ecological Niche Modeling Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the model transferability problem from ecological niche modeling to the challenge of validating genetic targets in non-European populations. In ecology, models trained in one region fail in another due to unaccounted-for environmental differences; similarly, genetic risk scores derived from European genomes often fail in other ancestries due to differences in linkage disequilibrium, allele frequency, and population-specific architecture. The analogous solution involves multi-region calibration and validation. Adapted here, the research would not simply replicate loci in one non-European cohort, but would design a framework to test PRS and locus associations across several diverse ancestry cohorts in parallel. It would quantify transferability metrics (e.g., variance explained, effect size correlation) for each population, diagnose the specific genetic or environmental factors causing poor performance in certain groups (akin to identifying limiting environmental variables), and use this insight to build next-generation, calibrated genetic models that are explicitly validated for use across a global diversity spectrum.

ideas.title: Develop cardiorenal protective drugs targeting reverse-causal pathways from subclinical CHD to kidney function
ideas.description: This research direction initiates a drug discovery program focused on the biological pathways that are activated when subclinical coronary heart disease (CHD) or its risk factors causally reduce eGFR. By first using genetic and molecular epidemiological tools to identify and validate these 'reverse-causal' pathways from heart to kidney, the project will then prioritize the most druggable mediators (e.g., specific inflammatory, fibrotic, or hemodynamic signals) for therapeutic intervention. The goal is to develop novel agents that protect kidney function by specifically blocking the detrimental signals originating from early cardiovascular pathology, rather than treating the kidney in isolation. This represents a paradigm shift from treating concurrent conditions to mechanistically interrupting a defined disease-driving axis.

ideas.title: Investigate sd-pQTLs in non-European ancestries
ideas.description: Extend the search for sex-differential genetic regulation of proteins to diverse ancestral populations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sex differences in the genetic regulation of the human plasma proteome

ideas.title: Allele-resolved causal loop detection for BP variants via haplotype-specific 3D editing
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond correlative population-level contact maps to establish causal links between non-coding BP risk variants and their target genes. It combines allele-specific chromatin conformation profiling in disease-relevant cellular models with precise CRISPR genome editing to functionally dissect specific chromatin loops. By deleting or inverting the variant-associated loop anchor and directly measuring the impact on both 3D structure and target gene expression, it tests whether the loop is necessary for gene regulation. This creates a direct experimental pipeline from physical connection to causal validation for specific risk haplotypes.

ideas.title: Instrumental Variable Methods with Explicit Pleiotropic Pathway Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core exclusion restriction assumption in Mendelian randomization (MR) by developing methods that explicitly model the pleiotropic pathway of a variant like rs653178, rather than assuming it away. The analogy comes from structural equation modeling (SEM) in psychology and social sciences, where researchers routinely handle variables with multiple causal pathways by specifying and estimating all plausible direct and indirect paths within a unified model. The adapted solution applies SEM frameworks to MR: the genetic variant is treated as an exogenous variable with at least two paths to the outcome—one mediated through the exposure of interest (the traditional MR path) and one direct (or mediated through an unmeasured confounder) representing pleiotropy. By estimating the strength of both paths simultaneously and using model fit indices to compare this expanded model against a restricted (no-pleiotropy) model, researchers can quantitatively assess the validity of the exclusion restriction for specific instruments and, when violated, still obtain consistent effect estimates by accounting for the pleiotropic pathway.

ideas.title: A multi-resolution causal signal concordance framework to dissect shared genetic architecture between eGFR and CHD
ideas.description: This study moves beyond standard colocalization to rigorously assess if eGFR and CHD share causal genetic variants. We implement a sequential analytical pipeline that first identifies independent genetic signals for each trait, then performs high-resolution fine-mapping to define credible sets of causal variants at each locus. The core analysis quantifies the overlap of these credible sets using a novel probabilistic metric that accounts for linkage disequilibrium and fine-mapping uncertainty. This multi-method framework provides a robust, nuanced view of shared genetic causality, distinguishing true pleiotropy from linkage effects and identifying loci with multiple independent or partially overlapping signals.

ideas.title: Calibrating Genetic Risk Scores via Cross-Population Transfer Learning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that SNP effect sizes (weights) for eGFR are generalizable across populations by drawing an analogy from climate science. In climate modeling, global models are calibrated to local conditions via bias correction and downscaling techniques to improve regional predictions. Similarly, we propose to treat a polygenic risk score built in one population as a potentially biased 'global model' for another. The adapted research idea is to develop a transfer learning or statistical calibration framework that uses genome-wide summary statistics from a target population to estimate systematic biases in the imported SNP weights. This method would 'downscale' the general score, correcting for population-specific genetic architecture, linkage disequilibrium, and environmental interactions to improve the accuracy and portability of eGFR genetic scores across diverse ancestries.

ideas.title: Quantify tissue-specific genetic contributions to systemic vascular physiology
ideas.description: This project moves beyond simple expression correlation to causally test the functional necessity of genes within specific non-vascular tissues for maintaining normal vascular physiology. Using inducible, tissue-specific gene perturbation (e.g., in neurons, renal tubules, or hepatocytes) in adult mice, it directly measures the quantitative impact on precise vascular phenotypes like hemodynamics and vessel mechanics. The design isolates the tissue-of-action by controlling for developmental effects and systemic metabolic feedback, enabling a rigorous comparison of contribution magnitudes across different organ systems. The output is a mapped network of non-vascular tissues ranked by their genetic leverage over vascular function.

ideas.title: A Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) Platform to Sustain Lifestyle Changes for High Genetic Risk of Hypertension
ideas.description: This research implements a randomized controlled trial of a digital JITAI system designed to overcome the central challenge of long-term adherence. High-genetic-risk participants are provided with a platform that uses data from wearables and smartphones to detect contexts of risk (e.g., high stress, inactivity, poor sleep) and deliver personalized, micro-interventions in real-time. The system dynamically tailors support based on individual adherence patterns and physiological responses, creating a closed-loop of measurement and intervention. The primary outcome is sustained engagement and physiological change over 24 months, testing whether this adaptive, technology-supported approach can durably modify lifestyle and offset genetic risk more effectively than static advice.

ideas.title: Functional triage of family-derived rare variants via multiplexed assay
ideas.description: This approach first performs deep sequencing in multiplex families to nominate a set of candidate rare variants within the loci of interest. These candidate variants, along with their native cis-regulatory sequences, are then synthesized into a library for a high-throughput functional screen, such as a massively parallel reporter assay (MPRA) in disease-relevant cell types (e.g., neuronal precursors). The screen quantitatively measures the regulatory impact of each variant, directly separating functional variants from neutral private polymorphisms. Finally, the highest-impact variants from the screen are validated using orthogonal methods like CRISPR-based editing in iPSC-derived models, establishing a direct link between genotype and molecular phenotype.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Tomography to Map BP Variant-Gene Connections
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from computer network diagnostics, specifically network tomography. In that field, the internal structure and performance of a network (like the internet) are inferred by sending probe packets from edge nodes and statistically analyzing the correlation patterns in their end-to-end delay or loss measurements, without direct internal access. We adapt this solution to the problem of linking distal blood pressure (BP)-associated variants to their target genes via chromatin capture. The adapted method involves applying a network tomography-like analytical framework to Hi-C or related chromatin interaction data. By treating chromatin contact maps as correlated measurements from a dynamic 3D network, we can statistically infer the most probable physical paths and functional connections between non-coding BP variants and promoter regions, even when direct Hi-C reads are sparse or noisy. This creates a probabilistically weighted interaction network that identifies robust variant-gene links across a population, improving the functional interpretation of GWAS hits for complex traits like hypertension.

ideas.title: Family Triangulation for Rare Variant Discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving framework from historical linguistics—specifically the comparative method used to reconstruct proto-words—to the challenge of finding rare causal variants in BP loci. In linguistics, scholars compare cognates across a language family, using established sound change laws to triangulate the original form and meaning. Adapted to genetics, we perform deep sequencing in multiple affected and unaffected members of families linked to a novel BP locus. By comparing sequences across the pedigree and applying the 'laws' of Mendelian inheritance and haplotype segregation, we can computationally triangulate the genomic origin of rare variants. This allows us to statistically reconstruct the most likely causal, high-impact variant by distinguishing true segregating risk alleles from background private mutations, effectively scaling down from a broad locus to a precise nucleotide change within a family context.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Correlation to Assess Shared Genetic Architecture
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where waveform cross-correlation is used to determine if seismic events share a common physical source. The analogous problem in genetics is determining if two trait associations in a locus stem from the same causal variant or are independent signals. The adapted research idea is to develop a fine-mapping cross-correlation method that compares the detailed 'genetic waveforms'—patterns of LD, association p-values, and effect sizes across SNPs in a genomic region—for eGFR and CHD. High correlation in these multivariate patterns would provide robust evidence for a shared causal genetic mechanism, moving beyond standard colocalization to assess the architectural similarity of the association signals themselves.

ideas.title: Applying Dose-Response Spline Methods from Toxicology to Model Non-Linear eGFR-CHD Effects
ideas.description: This idea challenges the linearity assumption in the causal effect of estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) on coronary heart disease (CHD) risk by drawing an analogy from toxicology. In that field, the linear no-threshold model for chemical risk is routinely challenged using flexible dose-response modeling (e.g., regression splines) to identify thresholds or non-linear relationships. The adapted solution applies this framework to epidemiology by using flexible modeling techniques within a Mendelian Randomization or rigorous observational study design. By fitting restricted cubic splines, fractional polynomials, or piecewise models to the eGFR-CHD relationship, the research can empirically determine if the causal effect is linear, exhibits a threshold (e.g., accelerated risk only below a specific eGFR), or follows another non-linear pattern (e.g., J-shaped), thereby refining risk prediction and clinical thresholds.

ideas.title: Apply single-cell eQTL mapping to BP loci using spatial transcriptomics for cellular and spatial localization
ideas.description: This idea adapts Species Distribution Modeling from Ecology to human genetics. In ecology, SDM maps a species to specific environmental niches within a geographic region. Here, we map genetic regulatory effects (eQTLs from blood pressure loci) to specific spatial and cellular niches within a vascular tissue sample. The solution is to integrate single-cell/nucleus RNA-seq for cellular resolution with spatial transcriptomics for positional context from human vascular tissues (e.g., arterial biopsies). By treating allele-specific expression as the 'occurrence data' and the cell's spatial coordinates, local cellular neighborhood composition, and proximity to tissue landmarks (e.g., vessel lumen, adventitia) as the 'environmental variables', we can build models to predict the spatial-cellular 'niche' where a genetic variant exerts its regulatory effect. This will localize BP-relevant eQTLs not just to a cell type, but to specific micro-anatomical domains and cellular interaction neighborhoods, providing unprecedented precision for mechanistic follow-up.

ideas.title: Challenge vascular tissue as primary site via cross-tissue functional mapping
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that vascular tissue is the primary site of action for genes associated with vascular pathologies. Drawing an analogy from Ecosystem Ecology and Keystone Species Theory, which solved the problem of identifying critical functional nodes by systematic removal experiments across an entire network, we adapt this solution. The adapted research involves a systematic, comparative functional screen: using inducible, tissue-specific genetic perturbations (e.g., Cre-lox systems), we will test whether ablating the function of candidate genes in non-vascular tissues (brain, kidney) produces an equal or greater phenotypic impact on vascular physiology than ablation in the vascular tissue itself. This cross-tissue functional mapping will empirically determine the primary site of action, challenging vascular-centric models of disease.

ideas.title: Adapting Geostatistical Kriging from Mining to Map Genetic-Environmental Risk Surfaces
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geostatistical kriging approach from mineral prospecting to human genetics. In the analogous field, kriging uses spatially referenced sample data and variogram models of spatial autocorrelation to interpolate ore grade across a continuous field. The adapted solution applies this to map population-level genetic risk for a complex trait like BP. By treating sampled genetic data (e.g., from biobanks) as 'ore samples' and incorporating local environmental measures as covariates, one can model how population genetic structure and environment jointly vary across space. The output is a predicted risk surface that identifies regions of elevated or diminished genetic-environmental risk, effectively transferring a spatial interpolation method from ecology/geology to generate novel, continuous maps of gene-environment interaction in human populations.

ideas.title: Apply Materials Science 'High-Throughput Screening' to Validate Novel GWAS Pathway Targets
ideas.description: Initiate drug discovery programs targeting the novel biological pathways implicated by GWAS. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where high-throughput experimentation rapidly screens vast libraries of novel material compositions, we propose adapting this solution to biological target validation. The adapted idea is to develop a parallelized, high-content screening platform that simultaneously tests the functional impact of perturbing multiple novel pathway components (via CRISPR, RNAi, or small-molecule probes) in diverse, disease-relevant cellular models. This approach will rapidly identify the most 'druggable' and critical nodes within these GWAS-implicated pathways, prioritizing the best targets for subsequent focused drug discovery campaigns and accelerating the translation of genetic findings into therapeutic programs.

ideas.title: Sparsity-Constrained Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of Plausible Epistatic Networks
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the intractability of modeling all possible epistatic interactions by first imposing a biologically informed sparsity constraint, limiting interactions to those supported by prior knowledge of molecular pathways and physical interactions. A hierarchical Bayesian model then estimates the strength of these predefined interactions, using regularization techniques to prevent overfitting. The model's architecture and parameters are rigorously validated for out-of-sample prediction on independent datasets. This yields a tractable, interpretable network of key epistatic effects that is both statistically robust and biologically plausible.

ideas.title: Model genetic loci as a complex interacting network using statistical physics methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of independent genetic loci by modeling their extensive epistatic interactions. The analogy is drawn from statistical physics, where complex systems with many interacting components (like spins in a magnet) are successfully modeled using network approaches such as the Ising or Potts models, which define explicit interaction parameters instead of assuming independence. The adapted solution is to treat the 107+ genetic loci as nodes in an interaction network, with edges parameterized by epistatic coefficients. By employing a Potts-like model and analysis techniques from statistical physics (e.g., mean-field theory, Monte Carlo simulations), the research would aim to simulate the joint state of this genetic network to predict phenotypic outcomes, capturing emergent properties arising from the system of interactions rather than from independent additive effects.

ideas.title: A Multi-Omics Data Fusion Framework Using Geophysical Data Assimilation Techniques
ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the multi-omics integration challenge by drawing an analogy from geophysical data assimilation used in weather forecasting. In that field, disparate, noisy observational data streams are fused with a dynamic physical model to create a consistent and accurate system state estimate. Adapted to genomics, we would develop a 'biological state assimilation' framework. A prior model based on known gene regulatory or signaling pathways would serve as the dynamic constraint. Sequential assimilation techniques (e.g., ensemble Kalman filters) would then integrate heterogeneous, layered omics data—GWAS variant associations, tissue-specific epigenomic marks, transcriptomic levels, and proteomic abundances—to continuously correct and update the model's state. This solution moves beyond simple statistical correlation, forcing integration through a mechanistic scaffold to produce a unified, causal, and dynamically refined view of how genetic variation propagates through molecular layers to influence phenotype in a specific tissue context.
